Now classify them by what the research actually IS:

Categories:
- basic_research: Understanding mechanisms, pathways, disease processes (knowledge output)
- biotools: Developing research tools, assays, methods, platforms (tool output)
- therapeutics: Developing drugs, treatments, therapies (treatment output)
- diagnostics: Developing clinical tests, biomarker panels (diagnostic test output)
- medical_device: Developing physical devices for patients (device output)
- digital_health: Patient-facing software, apps, clinical decision support
- other: Health services, behavioral interventions, epidemiology

application_id: 11200505
activity_code: F31
title: Positive Childhood Experiences and Intergenerational Trauma Transmission
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
Adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) exert a significant, detrimental impact on adult health outcomes,
while positive childhood experiences (PCEs) have been shown to have a protective effect, even in the
presence of ACEs. Less is known, however, about the specific mechanisms of intergenerational
transmission of parental ACEs and PCEs to their children’s ACEs and the impact on parenting practices.
The results of the proposed study will provide additional insight on parental transmission of both positive
and adverse childhood experiences, parenting practices, and opportunities to translate findings into
community interventions.

application_id: 10795652
activity_code: F32
title: A role for hypothalamic dopamine in predicting homeostatic need
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  In order to efficiently control energy homeostasis, the brain makes predictions about future caloric needs and food availability in order to invigorate food-seeking behavior. It is know that dopamine neurons encode reward prediction error signals that reinforce learning and guide the development of reward-seeking behavior. This proposal aims to investigate the role of dopamine in lateral hypothalamic area and test the hypothesis that LHA receives prediction error signals from the ventral tegmental area-revealing a novel feeding neural circuit and defining a new role for dopamine in feeding behavior.

application_id: 10782341
activity_code: R25
title: UPenn Post Baccalaureate Research Education Program.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative This program is designed to provide recent college graduates from populations underrepresented (UR) in the biomedical sciences with an intensive research experience, related enrichment activities, and academic advising for a period of one year, possibly longer. The goal of the program is to increase the number of students from UR backgrounds who enroll in a biomedical PhD program and pursue related careers. Ultimately, it is expected that UR biomedical researchers will introduce novel ideas and perspectives from their respective groups into the biomedical workforce, thereby aiding in the reduction of health care disparities.

application_id: 10994618
activity_code: F31
title: Functional Analysis of Pathogenic Human PTEN Variants in Early Neurodevelopment
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Neurodevelopmental disorders, including autism spectrum disorder, have increasing prevalence amongst children in the United States, yet there are no pharmacological treatments targeting the core symptoms. To understand the molecular mechanisms underlying these disorders, we must examine the function of high confidence risk genes, such as PTEN, in the developing brain. By uncovering the cellular and molecular changes effected by PTEN loss in neurodevelopment, we can identify targetable points for new pharmacological treatment to improve patient outcomes.

application_id: 10995018
activity_code: F31
title: A translational approach to interrogate the gut microbiome in sepsis-associated acute kidney injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Acute kidney injury in sepsis is common, costly, and responsible for high mortality and long-term morbidity. In preliminary studies of experimental sepsis and hospitalized patients, we have discovered that the gut microbiome plays an important but poorly understood role in sepsis-associated acute kidney injury. This project aims to understand how the gut microbiome contributes to the pathogenesis of kidney injury in sepsis, which will help provide insights that may lead to advances in its prevention and treatment.

application_id: 11312245
activity_code: K23
title: Music-based Intervention for Insomnia in Persons Living with Dementia and their Caregivers
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Untreated insomnia symptoms in community-dwelling persons with Alzheimer’s Disease and related Dementias contribute to increased institutionalization and accelerated disease progression. Because of the adverse side effects associated with drug treatment, effective, low-cost, easily adaptable in-home interventions are needed to improve insomnia symptoms in persons with Alzheimer’s Disease and related Dementias. Examining the effects of a tailored music-based mobile application aimed at improving insomnia symptoms in this population will inform future efficacy trials, potentially increasing the number of evidence-based options for non-pharmacologic approaches.

application_id: 10825080
activity_code: F31
title: Nicotinic modulation of excitatory synapse development in the prefrontal cortex
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In the US, 1 in 14 pregnant people smoke cigarettes during pregnancy; prenatal nicotine exposure leads to an increased risk of developing several neurobehavioral disorders (e.g., ADHD and autism spectrum disorders). Pressingly, the cellular and molecular mechanisms by which the nicotinic cholinergic system modulates circuit and synapse maturation, and how these are dysregulated by nicotine exposure during early brain development, remain poorly understood. Characterizing the role of the nicotinic cholinergic signaling in circuit and synapse maturation will expand our knowledge of brain development and elucidate therapeutic targets to mitigate the adverse outcomes of prenatal nicotine exposure.

application_id: 11160774
activity_code: R25
title: Comprehensive Advancement in Research Education and Training for Social Determinants of Health (CARES)
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: This R25 CARES program addresses gaps in social determinants of health (SDOH) education for nursing researchers, aiming to equip early career scholars with comprehensive SDOH research fundamentals and methodologies. The program includes short courses, summer institutes, mentorship, and networking activities over three years for three cohorts. By fostering a supportive community, enhancing research proficiency, and promoting research collaborations, the program seeks to empower nurse researchers to conduct impactful SDOH research, contributing to the advancement of knowledge in the field.

title: Genetic and epigenetic mechanisms of glioblastoma invasion and their therapeutic implication
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Glioblastoma (GBM) is the most aggressive type of malignant primary brain tumor and accounts for the majority of deaths among brain tumor patients due to its highly invasive nature and lack of therapeutics that efficiently target invasion. title: Understanding the Drivers of Antibiotic use in the Treatment of Childhood Diarrhea and Relationship to Antibiotic Resistance in China
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project focuses on antibiotic use for treatment of diarrheal disease among children and the relationship to the development of antibacterial resistance in rural China. With data from an audit study in clinics and pharmacies and a micro-level survey of households in southwest China, I will explore the drivers of antibiotic use, both on the supply side with clinicians and pharmacists and the demand side among parents and caregivers, using machine learning-based quasi-experimental statistical analysis and agent-based modeling to identify various drivers of antibiotic demand, to simulate the effects of alternative policies on antibiotic use and antibiotic resistance, and to identify promising policies and interventions for future study. intervention, Resilient Together for Dementia (RT-D), aimed at preventing chronic emotional distress and preserving
quality of life among dyads at risk for chronic emotional distress early after a diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease or a
related dementia (ADRD). Both persons living with dementia (PWDs) and their spousal care-partners experience high
levels of clinically elevated emotional distress, which can become chronic without treatment and negatively impact the
health, quality of life, communication, and care-planning of both partners. A tailored dyadic intervention delivered over
live video to this at risk population during the window of opportunity ...

application_id: 11094112
activity_code: F31
title: The role of the extracellular matrix in establishing Schwann cell polarity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
The development of the peripheral nervous system is critical for proper motor function and sensory perception;
however, loss of polarity in mouse Schwann cells leads to defects in timely myelination and Remak bundle
formation, which can contribute to peripheral neuropathies and neuropathic pain. By understanding the
interaction between extrinsic cues, from the extracellular matrix, and the internal state of Schwann cells, this
study will allow us to better understand how Schwann cells interact with their environment to regulate myelination
and Remak bundle formation.

application_id: 11244977
activity_code: F31
title: Glycopolymers as Cellular Glues
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The body’s natural defenses to prevent and clear infections rely in part on the ability of immune cells to
recognize glycans on the surface of pathogens. This project aims to develop glycopolymer “cellular glues” that
simultaneously bind to pathogens and immune cells. The formation of a complex between microbe, immune
cell, and “glue” is anticipated to stimulate immune cells, specifically macrophages and dendritic cells, and
improve various immune system functions, such as infection clearance and immune memory development.

application_id: 11251562
activity_code: F31
title: Characterizing Evoked Potentials of Deep Brain Stimulation for Parkinson's Disease
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) lead placement is a challenging and time-intensive procedure often requiring
patients to be awake to confirm motor symptom reduction and assess side effect profile intraoperatively.
Evoked potentials (EPs) hold promise as biomarkers to guide appropriate placement of DBS leads more
rapidly and accurately, possibly even in patients under anesthesia. In this way, the use of EPs as a biomarker
may reduce surgical time, improve patient outcomes, and improve patient comfort.

application_id: 11056175
activity_code: F31
title: Novel Spatial-Motor Approaches Targeting Post-Stroke Spatial Neglect and Gait Deficits
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Spatial neglect is a common symptom after stroke and negatively impacts the ability to achieve independent
functional mobility post stroke. This NRSA F31 proposal aims to explore and generate new information regarding
the combination of novel motor and spatial training approaches (prism adaptation therapy + electrical stimulation
and prism adaptation therapy + gait training) to enhance the rehabilitation of spatial neglect and walking function
in individuals post-stroke. This work will generate novel information that will inform future design of new and
targeted treatment protocols for evidence-based rehabilitation of spatial neglect and gait dysfunctions post-
stroke, to improve gait function and quality of life in stroke survivors.

application_id: 11113796
activity_code: F31
title: The role of experience during the critical period for the development of vocal motor circuits
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Vocal learners, like humans and songbirds, require experience with adults of their species while they are young
to learn and ultimately imitate their vocalizations, but how this early experience is encoded and interacts with
circuits for vocal production is not well understood. The essential song learning and motor production nucleus,
HVC, contains non-overlapping cell types that project to different regions. This research will elucidate if there
are cell-type-specific genetic and synaptic changes in HVC that underly sensory learning and how learning from
social experience interacts with motor circuits in HVC.

title: Mechanisms of Setd2 in cortical circuit function: Convergence with Fragile X Syndrome
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative:  Epigenetic regulation through the post-translation modification of histones is critical for normal neuronal function and epigenetic regulators have been implicated in neurodevelopmental disorders including autism spectrum disorder (ASD). activity_code: T32
title: Chemical Biology Training Interface
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY NEW YORK STONY BROOK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Chemical Biology Training Program at Stony Brook University trains students to work at the interface of
chemistry and biology and to apply quantitative skills to solve biomedical problems so that they can make major
contributions to the health and welfare of the United States. The training program provides students with skills
to communicate the significance of their work to the public and provides a venue for discussing career plans and
developing plans to successfully pursue their chosen career path. Training in chemical biology provides graduate
students with the chemical and biological methods necessary for developing new and improved diagnostics and
therapeutics.

application_id: 11378309
activity_code: F31
title: Neuronal Control of Motor State Transitions
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Difficulty initiating or executing appropriate movement is characteristic of neurological disorders including
Parkinson’s disease and ataxias, while abnormally repetitive movements are characteristic of neurological
disorders such as Tourette Syndrome and neuropsychiatric disorders such as OCD and ASD. This research is
aimed at understanding the neuronal mechanisms by which C. elegans nematodes initiate, execute and
stabilize appropriate motor actions, in order to make predictions about how dysregulation of motor action
patterns arise.

application_id: 11168682
activity_code: R25
title: San Diego State University Advancing Cancer Careers for ExceLlence (ACCEL)
org_name: SAN DIEGO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
San Diego State University’s Advancing Cancer Careers for ExceLlence (ACCEL) Program, in partnership with
University of California San Diego (UCSD) Moores Cancer Center, will provide 70 SDSU postdoctoral fellows
and graduate students each year with a coordinated and innovative array of resources to support excellence,
inclusion and transformation of the cancer research science force. ACCEL will leverage existing infrastructure
and develop new programming to a) expand and strengthen the pipeline of underrepresented (UR) scholars
pursuing cancer research; and b) broaden SDSU’s capacity to support and advance UR pre- and postdoctoral
cancer research scholars.

application_id: 10813710
activity_code: K23
title: Identifying Pathophysiological Signatures of DLB Cognitive and Neuropsychiatric Decline Using a Systems-Based Proteomic Approach
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) is a relatively common disorder that causes debilitating cognitive and psychiatric decline. No cure or effective therapies exist for these symptoms because the biological processes driving them are poorly understood. This project will analyze thousands of proteins in the brains and spinal fluid of individuals with DLB to better define its underlying biology and identify key proteins that can help us better monitor and treat this disease.

application_id: 11126575
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular mechanisms driving cessation of neural crest migration and aggregation into cranial ganglia
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
 The neural crest is an important stem-like cell population, known for its migratory behavior, and essential
for the normal development of numerous derivatives, such as the craniofacial skeleton, epidermal pigment cells,
and much of the peripheral nervous system. Abnormalities in the neural crest contributes to neurocristopathies
like familial dysautonomia as well as neural crest-derived cancers (i.e., neuroblastoma, pheochromocytoma,
melanoma). Further study of the neural crest and its development will lend insight into this unique population of
cells and give further indication of how irregularities in its development can contribute to these health conditions,
allowing for the development of targeted treatment.

title: Mechanisms of cell cycle regulation in embryos of normal and unusual size
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mammalian embryogenesis is a robust process with numerous checkpoints and corrective processes in place to ensure proper development. The proposed project sets forth to examine how morphogenesis regulates cell cycle remodeling during early postimplantation development and identify how this paradigm may contribute to cell number regulation in the mammalian embryo. Increased understanding of the details of developmental transitions in normal and perturbed conditions will reveal novel insight into the mechanisms regulating developmental robustness in mammalian embryos.

application_id: 11163835
activity_code: F32
title: Elucidating the Dynamics and Function of Long-Range Polycomb Interactions
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
The Polycomb repressive system is essential for proper regulation of gene expression and its dysregulation
leads to cancer and developmental defects. Yet, how this system establishes repression of target genes and
how these genes are derepressed in the correct spatiotemporal context during differentiation remain unknown.
The proposed research will use a multidisciplinary approach to reveal whether and how long-range 3D chromatin
interactions contribute to Polycomb-mediated gene repression, providing valuable insights into how cells are
able to precisely control gene expression during development and how disruption of Polycomb regulation leads
to human disease.

application_id: 11166557
activity_code: R25
title: Distance Education and Training on Emerging Contaminants and Technologies (DETECT)
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Despite notable advances in our understanding of hazardous compounds over the past century, there are many
compounds or hazards for which there is little or no regulatory guidance. As industrial hygienists and those in
related fields must often make immediate decisions regarding how to assess and manage these hazards on a
daily basis, it is imperative that they have access to the most current information available regarding
management of these hazards. In this project we will provide this information for prospective graduate students,
graduate students, and career professionals via development of online courses, online laboratories, and in-
person research training.

title: Inhibition of MIF nuclease-mediated parthanatos prevents neuron and oligodendrocyte death in the context of multiple sclerosis
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune, demyelinating, and degenerative disease characterized by lesions in the central nervous system that cause debilitating neurologic dysfunction. title: ROS as a somnogenic signal in the metabolic regulation of sleep

activity_code: F31
title: Control of retinal cell fate specification by the miR9-2 disease gene locus
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The nervous system is composed of cell classes of varied form, function, and genetic profile, yet often these cells originate from the same population of multipotent progenitors. Here, I propose to elucidate the genetic networks regulated by the microRNA, miR9-2 and its distal enhancer, using an in vivo miR9-2 knockout mouse model which has been found to have retinal structure perturbations. This project will fill a gap in our knowledge regarding the specific role of miR9-2 in cell types of the developing retina, its function as a genetic regulator, and its potential contribution to disease.

application_id: 11175334
activity_code: F31
title: The Role of the Neuronal Membrane Proteasome in the Peripheral Nervous System and Pain Sensation
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
The peripheral nervous system (PNS) is highly sensitive to disruption in activity of the proteasome, a protein
complex important for degradation and a range of neuronal regulatory activities, but the role of the newly-
discovered neuronal membrane proteasome (NMP) in the PNS is uncharacterized. The proposed experiments
seek to investigate: (1) the molecular structure of the PNS NMP and its distribution across neuronal plasma
membranes; and (2) PNS NMP regulatory activity in sensory neuron subpopulations and pain sensation. This
work will provide critical insight into the poorly-understood role of the proteasome in PNS pain signaling and
may reveal therapeutic targets for pain reduction or prevention of peripheral neuropathies.

title: Neonatal Functional Connectivity Associated with Callous-Unemotional Behaviors in Preschoolers
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Callous-unemotional traits, which are defined as deficits in empathy, prosociality, and guilt, are highly heritable and can be observed by 3 years of age. This study aims to examine whether the relationship between parent’s CU traits and children’s early-emerging CU traits is mediated by heritable changes in brain connectivity that are present at birth, prior to postnatal environmental exposures. This work may identify brain differences that precede the onset of callous-unemotional traits and are useful targets for earlier and more effective brain-based treatments.

activity_code: F32
title: Development of coarse-to-fine spatial processing throughout the primary visual system
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OREGON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Psychophysical studies show humans perceive low spatial frequency stimuli more quickly than high spatial
frequency stimuli, illustrating coarse-to-fine like processing. By studying the neural circuits and mechanisms underlying
the development of coarse-to-fine spatial frequency processing in mice, this project will add to our understanding of how
functional visual circuits develop through early postnatal life. The results of this project will shed new light on the circuits
mediating spatial vision and may be instructive for a complete understanding of developmental diseases such as amblyopia.

application_id: 10823888
activity_code: F31
title: Identification of a Putative Mitochondrial Solute Carrier that Regulates Mitophagy
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: Project Narrative Mitochondrial-selective autophagy (mitophagy) is an essential cellular process needed to promote cell survival, but the genes that play a role in this process are not well understood. I am studying a novel regulator of mitophagy at the mitochondrial surface that physically interacts with lipid transport protein VPS13D, a gene implicated in age-associated movement disorders that regulates mitochondrial clearance. Studying this process will benefit our overall understanding of mitophagy, and provide novel insight into therapeutics to combat defective mitophagy diseases like Parkinson’s disease.

application_id: 11109419
activity_code: K08
title: Early detection of bladder cancer through urothelial cell enrichment and DNA flow cytometry
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
Bladder cancer is the sixth most common cancer in the United States, but current screening tests have low
sensitivity, high cost, or require invasive procedures. My proposal will generate a non-invasive, low-cost
diagnostic workflow to detect bladder cancer, consisting of a novel method to enrich the cells that give rise to
bladder cancer from voided urine and a flow-cytometry assay to detect tumor-specific DNA changes in those
cells with high performance characteristics. The goal of this work is to improve outcomes of patients with
bladder cancer through the early detection of their disease, when it can be cured without removal of the
bladder.

Temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE) is a highly prevalent neurological disorder with associated cognitive

title: Understanding the functional anatomy of nociceptive spinal output neurons
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
Pain is a pervasive condition that affects millions of people worldwide. Spinal projection neurons in the superficial
laminae of the dorsal horn transmit the ascending pain signal from the spinal cord into the brain; however, the

anatomy, functional response properties, and behavioral role of spinal projection neurons to examine their
specific roles in nociception.

application_id: 10796868
activity_code: R25
title: Training Cancer Care Providers to Implement Sensitive Practice Guidelines When Treating Adult Survivors of Sexual Violence: A Blended Learning Approach
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cancer treatment providers – whether or not they are aware – encounter adult survivors of sexual violence in
the course of their practice, and yet few providers have the training needed to meet the unique treatment
needs of this group. The goal of the proposed R25E is to implement a blended learning program (E-Learning
plus Live Workshop) to train cancer care providers in research-based, sensitive practice techniques to help
reduce distress, increase feelings of safety and empowerment, and reduce triggering and retraumatization in
survivors of sexual violence undergoing cancer screening and treatment. Through this training, we hope to
improve the sensitivity of the cancer care offered to sexual violence survivors across the nation.

activity_code: F31
title: Non-Canonical Roles for Cell-Adhesion Molecules in Presynaptic Assembly
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Neurodevelopmental disorders, such as autism, lead to many impairments that negatively affect an individual’s quality of life. Because defects in synaptogenesis, and in particular mutations in cell-adhesion molecules, are often implicated in these disorders, it is imperative we understand how this biological process occurs. This project aims to understand how two cell-adhesion molecules contribute to synaptogenesis, furthering our understanding of the process and providing insights for possible treatments.

application_id: 10994923
activity_code: F31
title: Development of deep learning methods to optimize patient personalized treatment for craniosynostosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative Patients with craniosynostosis often require surgical correction but their treatment outcomes are highly variable and there are no methods to quantitatively characterize continuous process of head shape normalization after corrective surgery. Therefore, the choice of the most appropriate surgical procedure for each patient is highly dependent on expert qualitative evaluation and the experience of the clinical team. This proposal aims to quantify how patient-specific factors such as age, sex, and pre-surgical severity of anomalies affect head shape normalization after surgical treatment using a large retrospective dataset, to quantitatively compare outcomes between different surgical approaches, and to determine the optimal surgical approach personalized to each patient.

org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Exposure to traumatic events is associated with increased HIV risk in adults, however for young, forcibly displaced persons this relationship is less clear. application_id: 11030230
activity_code: F31
title: Exploiting KCNQ2 upstream open reading frames for therapeutic opportunity
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Genetic defects in the gene KCNQ2 can result in severe epilepsy affecting infants and children, but there are
currently no specific therapies that target this gene. In this proposal, we are focusing on a specific element in the
gene called an upstream open reading frame (uORF) that we discovered impairs optimal KCNQ2 translation into
protein. Our work will determine if removing or blocking this uORF, possibly with a new class of drugs called
antisense oligonucleotides, can boost levels of KCNQ2 protein in neurons as a potential strategy to overcome
the predisposition to seizures and epilepsy caused by genetic defects in this gene.

application_id: 11285356
activity_code: F31
title: Exploring the role of hyper-phagocytic microglia and SYK signaling in the aged hippocampus during COVID-19
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: Project Narrative
Since its emergence in 2019, SARS-CoV-2, a novel sarbecoronavirus, has infected at least half of the United
States population and has left many individuals with debilitating neurological post-acute COVID-19 sequalae
(neuro-PASC). Currently, the etiology of neuro-PASC remains unknown, but early clinical data suggests that
neuro-PASC could be attributed to an overactive immune system. Therefore, in line with these recent findings,
this proposed research project will determine whether microglia, the resident macrophages in the brain, rely
upon the spleen tyrosine kinase (SYK) gene, an important regulator of microglia inflammatory responses, to
perform pervasive activity in the COVID-19 brain.

application_id: 10995282
activity_code: F32
title: Investigation into transcriptomic control of dentate granule cell maturation and hyperexcitability in the apoE4 Alzheimer's Disease model
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The apoE4 gene is the strongest genetic risk factor for late onset Alzheimer’s disease and is implicated in early prodromal network dysfunction in the dentate gyrus of the hippocampus. Our preliminary work suggests an apoE4-induced accumulation of immature hyperexcitable dentate granule cells in aged mice. The proposed studies will use a combination of electrophysiology with single cell transcriptomics (patchSeq) together with morphological reconstruction to determine the mechanisms underlying apoE4-induced dentate gyrus dysfunction and how these mechanisms may be counteracted and treated.

application_id: 10897736
activity_code: F32
title: Exposure to PFAS mixture induces atherosclerosis via modulation of bile acid transport
org_name: WAYNE STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Exposure to the class of environmental toxicants known as per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) has been associated with increased risk factors for cardiometabolic disease, including hypercholesterolemia. The proposed research will investigate if PFAS exposure can therefore induce the development of atherosclerosis, while also seeking to understand mechanisms causing the atherosclerosis that are related to bile acid metabolism and transport.

application_id: 10995122
activity_code: F31
title: Mitochondrial dysfunction as a promoter of stria vascularis degeneration and inflammation in metabolic presbyacusis
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Degeneration of the stria vascularis within the inner ear leads to a major type of age-related hearing loss affecting hearing sensitivity and speech perception for many older adults. The proposed research will investigate how age-related changes in mitochondrial function within the stria vascularis impacts the inflammaging phenotype identified by cochlear macrophages. The results may highlight mitochondrial dysfunction as a key target aimed to ameliorate or slow the progression of chronic inflammation known to be associated with age-related hearing loss.

application_id: 10885119
activity_code: F32
title: Dissecting the mechanisms of how MYH7 S2 mutations lead to genetic hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Familial hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is a common genetic cardiomyopathy that leads to heart failure, but no specific disease-modifying therapy exists, even in cases with confirmed pathogenic variants. Breakthroughs in understanding of underlying mechanism are necessary to develop new therapies for treating HCM and new diagnostic tools to predict HCM risk and severity. In this proposal, I will use patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cells to interrogate mechanisms through which pathogenic myosin heavy chain-7 variants induce contractile dysfunction to increase our understanding of HCM’s pathogenesis and enable novel therapeutic and diagnostic approaches.

application_id: 11014966
activity_code: F31
title: Activity-Dependent Circuit Integration of Somatostatin Interneuron Subtypes in the Developing Neocortex
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative Recent work has uncovered incredible transcriptional diversity within the somatostatin cortical interneuron class. Although much work has shown that interneuron circuit integration drives the proper maturation of cortex, the precise efferent connectivity of cortical interneurons subtypes, and how this precision is achieved in development, is unknown. This project seeks to link transcriptional diversity of somatostatin subtype specific functional connectivity in cortical microcircuits, to examine how this specify arises in development and to explore the role of developmental activity in this process.

activity_code: K24
title: Towards Reduction of Harmful Overuse of Healthcare in Older Adults
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative My research agenda is to conduct patient-oriented research that advances understanding of overuse of healthcare among older adults. With the collaboration of junior investigators and other mentees, I will conduct research to understand how health care systems limit the harmful overuse of healthcare resources by their Medicare beneficiaries through their delivery of high quality primary care. application_id: 10795896
activity_code: F30
title: Defining the Pathogenesis of Del(7q) in Hematopoietic Stem Cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Loss of all or part of chromosome 7 is among the most common chromosomal abnormalities in high-risk myeloid diseases, yet the precise genetic and mechanistic contributions of these deletions remain unknown. There are many purported myeloid regulators and tumor suppressor genes on 7q, suggesting 7q may be a contiguous gene syndrome region in which the loss of multiple neighboring genes contributes to disease. This proposal aims to leverage CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing to model 7q deletions in vivo and identify combinatorial gene deficiencies that drive myeloid disease, and define the mechanisms by which dosage imbalances contribute to disease development.

application_id: 10825166
activity_code: F31
title: Determining a Central Locus for Temperature Modulation of Touch Sensitivity
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative This project aims to investigate how thermo-tactile interactions drive human behavior by studying touch and temperature perception together. We will use centrally-mediated thermal illusions to determine if illusory thermal cues and illusory thermal pain modulate vibrotactile perception, providing insight into whether these interactions also occur centrally. Additionally, we will examine if primary somatosensory neurons encode cutaneous thermal information by analyzing the neural firing rates in response to vibrations under varying skin temperatures in non- human primates.

application_id: 10646188
activity_code: F30
title: Decoding the Tissue of Origin of Cellular Damage from Cell-free DNA in Liquid Biopsies
org_name: GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) is released from dying cells into the circulation and can be detected through non-invasive liquid biopsy analysis. Decoding the cellular origins of cfDNA reveals changes in tissue homeostasis and altered cellular death rates due to tissue damage; thus, this approach is emerging as a powerful diagnostic resource for monitoring cancer dynamics, graft dysfunction in transplantation, prenatal testing and many other areas of medicine. In this proposal, I plan to track cellular damage after liver transplants and use this information to improve diagnosis and management of graft dysfunction that involves damage to multiple cell types, including donor-derived tissue-resident immune cells and other cell-types composing the liver.

title: Do Acquisitions of Hospice Agencies by Private Equity Firms and Publicly Traded Corporations Impact End-of-Life Quality and Cost of Care?
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative Concern has been raised by policymakers due to increasing private equity (PE) and publicly traded corporation (PTC) investment in hospice agencies and their purported effects on hospice care. application_id: 11211588
activity_code: F32
title: Impact of neurovascular coupling on receptor-mediated transport of proteins into the brain
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Active transport of proteins into the brain must be tightly and dynamically controlled to maintain neuronal function
and long-term health, yet little is known about how this transport is regulated. I will determine how neurovascular
coupling influences receptor-mediated transport into the brain by tracking the movement of fluorescently labeled
plasma proteins using two-photon imaging of the awake mouse brain. This research will contribute to
fundamental knowledge of the signaling mechanisms connecting blood-borne protein signals from the body to a
neuronal response in the brain, which could aid in the targeting of therapeutics for neurodegenerative diseases.

application_id: 11074031
activity_code: F31
title: Determining a Central Locus for Temperature Modulation of Touch Sensitivity
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative This project aims to investigate how thermo-tactile interactions drive human behavior by studying touch and temperature perception together. We will use centrally-mediated thermal illusions to determine if illusory thermal cues and illusory thermal pain modulate vibrotactile perception, providing insight into whether these interactions also occur centrally. Additionally, we will examine if primary somatosensory neurons encode cutaneous thermal information by analyzing the neural firing rates in response to vibrations under varying skin temperatures in non- human primates.

application_id: 10997249
activity_code: F31
title: Control of Axon Degeneration by Palmitoylation
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Degeneration of neuronal axons occurs after acute nerve injury and in several neuropathological conditions, including chronic neuropathies. Several proteins that control axon degeneration are modified with a lipid called palmitate, a process called palmitoylation. The proposed research will define the importance of palmitoylation for axon degeneration and could reveal new therapeutic strategies to prevent axon degeneration in a wide range of neuropathologies and thereby benefit human health.

This project aims to investigate how thermo-tactile interactions drive human behavior by studying touch and

application_id: 11065822
activity_code: F31
title: Investigation of the Lipid Transfer ProteinVPS13D at the Golgi Apparatus
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  VPS13D is a member of the VPS13 bridge-like lipid transfer protein family and is implicated in the neurodegenerative disease spastic ataxia. It has multiple localizations including at the Golgi apparatus, yet its function within the Golgi remains completely unknown. My aim to investigate the precise localization and function of VPS13D at Golgi will provide knowledge relevant to both fundamental cell biology and to the field of neurodegenerative diseases.

application_id: 11054633
activity_code: T32
title: Redox Biology and Medicine Training Program
org_name: WAKE FOREST UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Redox biology and medicine, the theme of this predoctoral training program at Wake Forest University for which funding is requested, denotes the specialized chemistry of oxidation and reduction occurring in biological systems which orchestrates metabolism and signaling to elicit specific cellular responses. Redox mechanisms support well-being and are also involved in the development and progression of many diseases. The goal of this training program is to bring scientists from chemistry, physics and molecular medicine together to train in the performance of interdisciplinary redox research to improve human health.

application_id: 11016937
activity_code: F31
title: Characterizing novel Mixed Etiology Dementia models compromised for TDP-43 function
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative TDP-43 pathology, due to loss of TDP-43 nuclear function, is a hallmark of several neurodegenerative diseases including mixed etiology dementias (MEDs) such as Alzheimer’s Disease with TDP-43 pathology (AD-TDP) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD). Proposed studies will elucidate the role of TDP-43 in the pathological conversion and “prion-like” spreading of tau protein in the presence or absence of ß-amyloidosis. These mechanistic insights can guide the development of novel therapeutic approaches for MEDs currently without effective treatment.

application_id: 10996492
activity_code: F31
title: Glycopolymer Biomaterials for Regulating Adaptive Reprogramming in Glia
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: When the central nervous system is disrupted by injury or neural device implantation, local glial cells deprioritize normal, healthy functions in favor of reactive phenotypes that assist in the resolution of the injury. Saccharide- based materials offer the ability to regulate this glial adaptive reprogramming response both by leveraging natural biofunctionality of the glycans and by controlled presentation of chemical and mechanical stimuli. In this project, I will develop polysaccharide biomaterials to (1) test the ability of inherently bioactive glycans to stimulate glia- based repair following stroke, and (2) establish synthetic glial interfaces that allow for the phenotypic, functional, and transcriptomic characterization of adaptive reprogramming.

application_id: 10731731
activity_code: F32
title: Analysis of transcriptional priming of progenitors in the vertebrate neural plate border
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative The neural plate border is the site of specification for diverse cell types including neural crest and ectodermal placode cells, and cells of the central nervous system (CNS). Despite the involvement of neural plate border cells and their derivatives in human health and disease—craniofacial and cardiovascular defects, cancers including melanoma and neuroblastoma, and congenital deafness can all result from their dysfunction—a detailed understanding of their early specification and lineage commitment is lacking. The goal of this proposal is to investigate the epigenetic regulation of miRNAs during specification of neural plate border cells in order to understand how this diverse pool of progenitors becomes differentially primed for the acquisition of distinct fates.

application_id: 10935093
activity_code: T32
title: The New Houston Area Molecular Biophysics Program
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
The Houston Area Molecular Biophysics Program prepares PhD students for research applying the principles
of physics to important medical problems and to the basic biology underlying medical research. Training in
Molecular Biophysics and the quantitative and interdisciplinary skills emphasized in the program enable
research careers leading to fundamental understanding of human disease and development of innovative
approaches to diagnostics, therapeutics, and disease prevention. Biophysics provides tools used by all
specialties in biomedical research and in medical practice.

application_id: 11251331
activity_code: F31
title: The Effect of Social Isolation on Inhibitory Modulation of Tactile Processing
org_name: SALK INSTITUTE FOR BIOLOGICAL STUDIES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Sensory feedback is critical for effective interaction with the world, requiring neural circuit mechanisms for
dynamically adjusting how sensory signals are transmitted as conditions and contexts change. This proposal
aims to define how connections between the central amygdala, a structure involved in processing internal states
of stress and fear, and the cuneate nucleus, which transmits tactile information, are involved in adjusting the
strength of sensory signaling on short time scales as well as following stressful experiences such as chronic
isolation. Identifying the neural mechanisms by which social isolation modulates the processing of sensory
feedback will help to clarify how chronically altered internal states affect the nervous system and lay the
foundation for ...

application_id: 11143615
activity_code: F31
title: The role of extracellular vesicles as a novel trophic signaling platform in nervous system development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: Project Narrative
Signaling between cells is essential for the proper development and function of neural circuits and occurs often
over long distances. This project will test the hypothesis that extracellular vesicles are a novel long-distance
mechanism of intercellular neurotrophin signaling which promotes recipient neuron survival. Ultimately, this work
will increase our understanding of the mechanisms required for the proper target-directed circuit refinement in
the developing nervous system.

activity_code: F30
title: Defining the genetic mechanisms of ETS1 dependency in Notch-activated T Cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (T-ALL)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Over 60% of T cell acute lymphoblastic leukemias (T-ALLs) harbor mutations in the NOTCH1 signaling pathway; however, pan-Notch inhibitors are associated with significant toxicities. We previously showed that the lymphoid- restricted transcription factor ETS1 is essential for Notch-activated T-ALL survival, but not essential for overall health. Here, we propose a study to define the downstream oncogenic pathways and cis-regulatory elements of ETS1 in T-ALL, which may lead to new anti-leukemic strategies without intolerable toxicities.

application_id: 10824645
activity_code: F31
title: Single-cell optical phenotyping for diffuse gliomas using artificial intelligence and label-free microscopy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Label-free optical microscopy, combined with artificial intelligence (AI), has emerged as a promising method for the rapid imaging of fresh, unprocessed surgical specimens, bedside brain tumor diagnosis, margin delineation, and molecular genetic prediction. Existing AI methodologies are limited because they rely on weak, slide- or patient-level annotations for model training, and are therefore unable to capture the cellular complexity and spatial heterogeneity found in diffuse gliomas. The objective of this research is to develop an innovative AI-based system to learn single-cell optical image features and identify patient-level clusters, without weak supervision, in order to advance the role of AI in diffuse glioma research and enable better, and more personalized care.

application_id: 11190777
activity_code: F31
title: An Evolutionarily Conserved RNA Binding Protein Critical for Proper Neuronal Function Regulates Lipid Storage and Metabolic Pathways
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Intellectual disability, characterized by an I.Q. less than 70 and significant impairments in intellectual and
adaptive behaviors, affects approximately 1-3% of the world population. Despite the heterogeneous and
genetically complex nature of this condition, recent studies have led to the discovery that many genes linked to
intellectual disabilities are unified by common underlying molecular dysfunctions. In these proposed studies, I
use the fruit fly Drosophila as a system to model a heritable form of intellectual disability, which allows me to
explore the brain-metabolism connection in development and disease.

application_id: 11049030
activity_code: R25
title: Emory Psychiatry Clinical Scientist Training Program (CSTP)
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Addressing the significant mental health burden of our modern society cannot be accomplished in the absence
of meaningful scientific discovery by physician-scientists. The Emory Clinical Scientist Training Program
(CSTP) addresses the critical shortage of well-trained physician-scientists in psychiatry by utilizing an
innovative research training platform, grounded in state-of the-art clinical neuroscience research and
addressing the primary need for intensive mentorship throughout psychiatry residency. In addition, the Emory
CSTP provides research training and experiences to all psychiatry residents to nurture their interests in
research and prepare them for a life-long practice of evidence-based medicine.

application_id: 10897225
activity_code: K23
title: Estimating the Contribution of Alcohol and Metabolic Risk to Liver Disease Progression to Inform Personalized Interventions
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alcohol- (ALD) and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) constitute the majority of liver-related morbidity and mortality. Based on national trends of increasing obesity and harmful alcohol use, the health burden of NAFLD and ALD is projected to rise, particularly among women, young adults, and under-represented minorities. This proposal directly addresses these overlapping epidemics through the lens of liver fibrosis (the most significant predictor of patient outcomes in ALD and NAFLD) by examining: (i) the longitudinal interactions of alcohol and metabolic risks on liver fibrosis, aiming to provide more individualized prognostic information; (ii) proprotein convertase subtilisin kexin type 9 (PCSK9), a novel biomarker and potential therapeutic target in ALD.

application_id: 10953092
activity_code: R36
title: Relationship between sleep disturbances, vasomotor symptoms, and cognitive health in Black and Latina midlife women
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Rates of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) are higher in women and in Black and Hispanics; and identifying modifiable risk factors in this population is a priority in lowering the burden of AD. Compared to white women, Black and Hispanic women experience worse menopause symptoms, including hot flashes, sleep disturbance, and cognitive difficulties. The goal of this study is to determine whether sleep disturbance, hot flashes and sleep- wake activity are linked to memory performance in Black and Hispanic menopausal women, and therefore could represent midlife risk factors for cognitive decline and AD.

application_id: 11252870
activity_code: F31
title: Effects of the NR2E1 mutation on cellular and molecular determinants of neurogenesis
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
The decline of neurogenesis has been associated with many neurological disorders. Considering the critical
role NR2E1, a nuclear receptor, plays in regulating neurogenesis, it is likely that certain NR2E1 missense
mutations might cause neurogenesis-associated pathology by impairing NR2E1 function and/or activity. Our
project will investigate the molecular, cellular, and behavioral consequences of one patient-specific NR2E1
mutation to gain insight into the importance of NR2E1 for human neurogenesis.

activity_code: T32
title: Cross-disciplinary training in computational approaches to the neuroscience of audition and communication
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Hearing loss is commonplace: (1) 2-3 children out of every 1000 born in the USA have some form of hearing loss and (2) by the age of 75, over 50% of adults have disabling hearing loss. Language impairments, such as aphasia, also affect a significant number of Americans: 150,000+ each year. Thus, the proposed training program is extremely relevant to public health because it will help identify, motivate, and train the next generation of auditory and communication neuroscientists to exploit the explosion in computational and theoretical techniques from the very earliest stages of their scientific careers so that they can help lead the way to better diagnoses and treatments of a wide range of hearing and communication disorders.

title: Regulation of Presynaptic Gene Transcripts During Synaptogenesis
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Neuronal mRNA dysregulation is associated with neurodevelopmental diseases and axon defects, yet the roles and mechanisms of mRNA regulation in presynaptic assembly remain poorly understood. This project takes advantage of the genetic accessibility and transparency of the nematode C. elegans to investigate mRNA regulation as synaptogenesis occurs in the living animal, focusing on the conserved essential presynaptic active zone gene syd-2/liprin-alpha. Studying mRNA regulation in vivo using complementary high-resolution live imaging and unbiased biochemical approaches will identify both the precise biological roles these processes play in synaptic function and identify novel regulators for future therapeutic targeting.

application_id: 11159363
activity_code: F31
title: Interrogation of Non-Canonical Roles of VGSCs in Neurodevelopmental Epilepsy
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Voltage-gated sodium channels (VGSCs) mediate action potential propagation and transduction
in neurons, however, recent work has shown that VGSCs serve additional functions in non-
excitable cell types and in the developing nervous system. The potential contribution of these
“non-canonical” functions of VGSCs to the development of neurodevelopmental conditions
remains unknown. This proposed work will use human induced pluripotent stem cell-derived
brain organoids and genome engineering to investigate the molecular mechanism underlying
non-canonical VGSC contributions to neurological development and disease.

application_id: 10901570
activity_code: F31
title: Contribution of Levodopa to Brain Iron Accumulation
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Parkinson’s disease (PD) patients suffer from elevated levels of brain iron with levodopa (L-DOPA) commonly used by clinicians as a first-line antiparkinson drug. This project will research how our standard administration of L-DOPA impacts critical properties of iron homeostasis with the goal of identifying key iron mechanisms in the blood-brain barrier which will be confirmed by utilizing an in-vitro blood-brain barrier model and in-vivo murine model. This research will allow for the understanding of the mechanism of iron transport after L-DOPA exposure and target critical regulators to reduce brain iron accumulation and improve antiparkinson drug therapy development.

title: Molecular mechanisms of Kv1 channel localization at the axon initial segment
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The axon initial segment is the site of action potential generation and requires high densities of voltage-gated ion channels to carry out this function. phr: Project Narrative  The proposed project will investigate the transcriptomic contributions of human and non-human primate brain development. application_id: 10994420
activity_code: F31
title: Defining the function of a novel insulin-like growth factor binding protein in CNS development and function
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Neurodevelopmental disorders encompass a range of intellectual, social, and behavioral deficits, and are increasingly prevalent in the child population. Functional insulin/insulin-like growth factor (IGF) signaling is required for proper brain growth and function and, thus, is closely regulated by IGF binding proteins. Since dysregulation of IGF signaling is strongly linked to developmental diseases, this project seeks to uncover regulatory mechanisms of IGF binding proteins in central brain circuit formation and function while exploring potential avenues for therapeutic intervention in neurodevelopmental disorders.

application_id: 10995008
activity_code: F30
title: Characterizing host proteins that restrict Zika virus
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Type I interferon is a signaling molecule, produced and secreted upon a cell’s detection of virus, that creates an antiviral state in nearby cells to prevent further infection. AMOTL2 is a gene with no previously described antiviral role that appears to contribute to the type I interferon response against Zika virus. A better understanding of the mechanism and breadth of AMOTL2’s antiviral phenotype will improve our basic understanding of the type I interferon response against viruses.

application_id: 11192749
activity_code: F31
title: Contractile Pericyte-Mediated Cerebrovascular Deficits in a Mouse Model of CADASIL
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Project Narrative
CADASIL (Cerebral Autosomal Dominant Arteriopathy with Subcortical Infarcts and Leukoencephalopathy)
patients often experience strokes and cognitive impairment, and while disease causing mutations in the protein
NOTCH3 have been studied deeply in brain blood vessels, a key cell type that envelops brain blood vessels
known as the pericyte is virtually unstudied in this disease despite ample NOTCH3 presence. The goal of this
proposal is to improve our understanding of pericyte contributions to early brain blood flow deficits seen in this
disease as pericytes contribute to multiple blood flow control mechanisms. Determining the role that pericytes
play in disease progression can lead to identification of targets for early therapeutic intervention.

title: G-RISE at Delaware State University: Diversifying neuroscience through predoctoral training at an HBCU
org_name: DELAWARE STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Public Health Relevance
Today’s biomedical researchers represent the backbone of innovation that improves public health

at Delaware State University (DSU) will promote diversity of the new workforce in biomedical research.

research workforce.

application_id: 11249110
activity_code: F31
title: Understanding the Role of Histone Ubiquitination in Plasticity and Neurodevelopmental Disease
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Mutation to WW-domain containing adapter with coiled-coil (WAC), an adapter in the deposition of histone H2B
ubiquitination (H2Bub), leads to DeSanto-Shinawi Syndrome, a neurodevelopmental disorder with autism-like
characteristics. The proposed studies will leverage genomic and behavioral analyses to define the role of WAC
in H2Bub-dependent gene regulation in neurons, exploring the hypothesis that WAC facilitates proper neuronal
activity-dependent transcription. This work will establish H2Bub as a novel epigenetic regulator of neuronal
activity and provide insight into the molecular etiology of neurodevelopmental disorders driven by dysfunction of
H2Bub regulators.

application_id: 10997698
activity_code: F31
title: Determining how a single-residue PKR mutation found in patients with familial dystonia causes aberrant PKR activation
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative 1. PKR is a pattern recognition receptor which binds dsRNA, and that is overactivated in a variety of debilitating neurologic diseases (including Huntington’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, and Aicardi-Goutières syndrome). 2. The G130R mutation in PKR leads to enhanced PKR activation both in patient fibroblasts with familial neurologic disease as well as in our cell culture system, but the mechanism of overactivation is not known. 3. This proposal will attempt to identify the cause of PKRG130R overactivation and its role in causing neurodegeneration, which may help identify strategies for future therapeutics for a range of different neurologic diseases where PKR is overactive.

activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Neutrophil Heterogeneity and Molecular Mechanisms in Driving ALS
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, otherwise known as ALS or Lou Gehrig's disease, is a neurodegenerative disease leading to progressive paralysis and death that has few viable treatment options. We now know that immune cells, particularly neutrophils, play an important role in ALS, but the manner in which neutrophils contribute to disease progression is currently unclear. This proposal will identify types of neutrophils that are dysregulated in ALS, the mechanisms driving neutrophil activity in ALS, and the way these groups and mechanisms contribute to ALS progression, ultimately leading to new treatment options.

Properly studying a single organ like the skin now requires a multidisciplinary approach that considers not only

into one (e.g., neuroimmunology). This Training Program in Systems Skin Biology aims to build on the
existing institutional strengths of Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai (ISMMS) to create an intersectional
science training program for both predocs and postdocs in Systems Skin biology.

title: Role of KCC2 in sympathetic dysfunction after spinal cord injury
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Over 300,000 people in the United States suffer from spinal cord injury (SCI). Heightened sympathetic

title: Hypoxia-Induced Misdirection: Understanding Postnatal Inhibitory Interneuron Migration in Neonatal Hypoxic Injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The development of the infant brain is a dynamic, intricate process that can be disrupted by oxygen
deprivation, a frequent cause of long-term neurological disability. This proposal will generate gene expression
profiles from normal and hypoxic human neonatal brain tissues and establish a piglet in vitro system to model
effects of reduced oxygen levels on the developing infant brain. The findings from this research will enhance
our understanding of neonatal hypoxic injury and guide future efforts towards therapeutic interventions.

application_id: 11262177
activity_code: F31
title: Neural dynamics of freely-moving, naturalistic behavior
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
 Movement is essential to our everyday lives, giving us the freedom to interact with a rich, complex world
around us. This proposal seeks to understand how motor cortical dynamics generalize from supporting artificially
constrained behavior to freely-moving, naturalistic behavior, allowing for more real-world applications to be trans-
lated from the basic scientific findings in the motor systems field. This work has applications for understanding
how the cortical control of natural, everyday motor functions can be disrupted in people with stroke or traumatic
brain injury and can help optimize the decoding of naturalistic movements for assistive neurotechnology, such as
neural prosthetics, for those with motor disabilities to restore the freedom of movement.

title: Precision Monitoring of Multiple Sclerosis with Mobile Devices: A Transformative Approach
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Multiple Sclerosis is a neurodegenerative disease that is tracked and assessed using infrequent and expensive
clinical assessments and imaging studies. Capturing extra-clinical disease activity at higher frequencies using
new sensors and capabilities on mobile devices can improve clinical decision making, and improve clinical
researchers monitor disease activity. This work aims to develop the models and practices necessary to
remotely track MS disease activity using mobile device data, and unlock our understanding of what happens
outside of the clinic, thus having the potential to improve the care of patients and quality of MS research
worldwide.

application_id: 11068942
activity_code: F31
title: Causal Inference in Bimanual Touch
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative While bimanual touch is essential to our daily lives, it is unknown how the nervous system integrates touch over the hands to arrive at a sensory percept. In this proposal, I will use virtual reality and human neuroimaging to test the hypothesis that bimanual touch is integrated within a Bayesian Causal Inference framework. Understanding how touch is integrated across the hands will advance our understanding of broader principles of sensory integration and the development of novel therapies for sensory-related disorders.

title: Dynamic Role of Hyaluronan in Ventricular Remodeling
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF LOUISVILLE
phr: Project Narrative Hyaluronan is a key component of organs and may play a role in wound healing; however, the role of hyaluronan in the failing heart is not known. application_id: 11115226
activity_code: K08
title: Carbonic Anhydrase Mediated Regulation of Microtubule Dynamics and Infection in Cystic Fibrosis
org_name: LURIE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a genetic, life-shortening disease most often due to respiratory failure, with chronic lung
infections presenting a major challenge. This study explores how disrupted microtubule dynamics and
intracellular transport, mediated by reduced carbonic anhydrase activity, contribute to increased infections in
CF. Understanding these mechanisms may reveal new therapeutic targets to prevent and treat infection in
people with CF and other airway diseases.

application_id: 10995615
activity_code: F31
title: Digital phenotypes from a low-cost smartwatch to inform early detection of Alzheimer's disease and related dementias
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE As the aging US population increases and interventions for dementia become available for early stages, there is a growing need for low-cost sensitive tools to detect early cognitive decline in large samples. Digital phenotyping, a novel approach involving passive data collection from sensors on personal devices, such as smartwatches, could fill this gap to estimate early autonomic dysfunction and health behaviors associated with early cognitive decline. This project will examine the validity of several metrics from a commercially available and low-cost smartwatch in a diverse sample of older adults, which could have broad implications for health behavior monitoring, early detection of cognitive decline, and targeted interventions.

activity_code: T32
title: NUTRITION TRAINING
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Over the past 45 years, the NIDDK-supported training program in the Division of Nutritional Sciences has been
instrumental in the development of nutrition professionals who hold research positions in academia,
government, and industry, and key policy positions in government, international agencies, and nonprofit
foundations. The training program is built on the graduate program in nutrition at Cornell University that
emphasizes multidisciplinary and integrative scholarship across the biological, physical, behavioral,and social
sciences, as appropriate for individual trainees. The NIDDK-supported training program emphasizes
translational and collaborative approaches to meet national needs in translational research and includes a
combined PhD-RD training program.

title: Intensive Longitudinal Associations between Stress, Mindfulness, and Alcohol Use in Highly Stressed Mothers
org_name: GEORGE MASON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative This study uses ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to investigate momentary associations between stress and alcohol craving and use in a group of highly stressed mothers of adolescents and whether mindfulness moderates this association. The goal of this study is to identify targets for interventions that may disrupt associations between stress and drinking. Findings from this study will be used to inform interventions, including smartphone-based just-in-time interventions, for alcohol-related problems that may be tailored to meet the needs of women who have been underrepresented in alcohol literature and who have unique social demands and stressors (e.g., parenting).

application_id: 11258401
activity_code: F31
title: Causal Inference in Bimanual Touch
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
While bimanual touch is essential to our daily lives, it is unknown how the nervous system integrates touch over
the hands to arrive at a sensory percept. In this proposal, I will use virtual reality and human neuroimaging to
test the hypothesis that bimanual touch is integrated within a Bayesian Causal Inference framework.
Understanding how touch is integrated across the hands will advance our understanding of broader principles
of sensory integration and the development of novel therapies for sensory-related disorders.

application_id: 11295367
activity_code: F31
title: The role of subsynaptic nanoscale architecture in inhibitory synaptic plasticity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
GABAergic inhibitory synapses mediate synaptic inhibition in the brain by regulating neuronal excitability, cell
firing, dendritic integration, and synaptic plasticity – mechanisms which are critical for learning, memory, and
cognition, and are disrupted in disease. The goal of this project is to investigate the fine details of synapse
organization to understand how nanoscale subsynaptic architecture contributes to functional synaptic plasticity.
This will begin to identify molecular interactions and structural mechanisms involved in the function and plasticity
of inhibitory synapses and offer clues to how they are disrupted during disease.

title: Disease mechanism and genetic therapy for a pathogenic STXBP1 variant
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE

common missense variant in STXBP1 encephalopathy, and lack of knowledge about the mechanism underlying

options for STXBP1 patients are symptom specific and limited, this project presents the opportunity to establish
a potential therapeutic for patients with missense variants in STXBP1. This project is applicable to the NIH’s

application_id: 11089348
activity_code: T32
title: NIDA Training Program in Neuroscience
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Addiction is a major public health concern as it affects millions of individuals afflicted with the disorder, and has
negative consequences for society at large. Great advances have been made in understanding the actions of
drugs of abuse on the brain, but much remains to be learned. The purpose of this training grant is to foster
interest in addiction among graduate students supported, such that these students will devote their research
and eventually their careers to the study of the neurobiological bases of addiction and development of effective
treatments.
terms: <NIDA><National Institute of Drug Abuse><National Institute on Drug Abuse><Neurosciences><Training Programs>

application_id: 11075343
activity_code: R25
title: Stanford Undergraduate Summer Cardiovascular Research Program
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Many populations experience a significantly higher risk of death or disability from heart disease or stroke, and it is therefore important to foster a cardiovascular research and care workforce that reflects the US population. The goal of this R25 program is to increase the numbers of individuals pursuing careers in cardiovascular medical research by providing undergraduate students from a broad range of backgrounds with a short-term mentored research experience in the laboratories of Stanford Medicine’s Cardiovascular Institute. Participation in mentored research increases the degree to which students identify as scientists, which in turn increases the likelihood that students persist in research careers.

application_id: 10784686
activity_code: K08
title: Nanoparticle-Enhanced Radiation Therapy for DIPG
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative DIPG is one of the most lethal pediatric brain cancers, affecting children aged 5-9 yrs old, without a current cure or significant improvement in the efficacy of treatment over the past 3+ decades. With the promise of improving quality of life, improving the safety of radiation, or increasing length of survival for children with DIPG, nanoparticle-enhanced radiation therapy first requires validation in a pre-clinical DIPG model, and additional mechanistic investigation. The included project proposal is focused on the pre-clinical development and refinement of this technique.

application_id: 11067037
activity_code: F31
title: In Vivo Measurement of Human Brain Tissue, Blood, and CSF Dynamics Supporting Glymphatic Function
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Healthy brain function requires removal of waste products, which typically occurs naturally, and impairment of this brain clearance is associated with many diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease and other forms of dementia. Our understanding of the function and dysfunction of this clearance process limited, partly due to a lack of tools to observe this process in humans and understand how it fails. Here, we propose a novel Magnetic Resonance Imaging method that can observe aspects of brain clearance and use it to test a hypothesis that clearance is in part driven by dynamic changes in brain blood vessel size and subsequent motion of brain tissue and the surrounding cerebrospinal fluid.

title: Epigenetic Regulation of Retinal Development by BCOR
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Regenerative medicine in the retina is an attractive solution for improving quality of life in patients affected by neurodegenerative diseases and depends on our knowledge of retinal development. Polycomb-group (PcG) complexes are evolutionarily conserved, powerful epigenetic machineries that regulate cell-fate decisions, and here, we identified retinal multipotency genes as novel targets of the PcG protein BCOR. application_id: 10885699
activity_code: K08
title: Lung fibrosis impairs tumor immunity and limits immune checkpoint efficacy
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Immune checkpoint inhibitors, either alone or in combination with cytotoxic chemotherapy, are standard treatments for patients with metastatic lung cancer, yet less than half of patients will experience a reduction in size of their tumors and these responses typically last less than one year. This limited efficacy is in part due to an immunosuppressive fibrotic microenvironment characterized by impaired T cell function as well as dysfunctional antigen presentation. We will identify mechanisms by which TGFb inhibition in combination with chemotherapy can restore anti-tumor immunity to enhance immune checkpoint blockade.

application_id: 10898321
activity_code: F30
title: Dynamic reprogramming of macrophage acetyl-CoA metabolism in endotoxin tolerance
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Macrophages’ precise regulation of inflammation is vital in maintaining homeostasis and preventing excessive or insufficient inflammation observed in critical illnesses like sepsis, affecting 1.7 million US adults annually. One method of control is endotoxin tolerance, which is the ability for a secondary innate immune response to be diminished to prevent a hyperinflammatory or persistent response upon repeated exposure to endotoxins. This project aims to investigate the role of dynamic macrophage metabolism reprogramming in modulating the inflammatory response during endotoxin tolerance, potentially uncovering novel metabolic targets for managing critical illnesses such as sepsis and promoting human health.

title: Neural Mechanisms of Behavioral Variability and Strategy Selection in Larval Zebrafish
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Selecting appropriate actions according to context is a complex task that all animals must perform in order to survive. In this project, I will use larval zebrafish to characterize how activity in the brain gives rise to trying alternative behaviors when current actions are not working and to investigate the neural mechanisms underlying the strategy switch from exploring different actions to repeating a stereotyped movement. activity_code: F31
title: Modeling red blood cell metabolism in health and disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Understanding the metabolism of human red blood cells (RBCs), the most abundant cell in the body is crucial
for improving transfusion medicine and understanding diseases caused by inborn errors of metabolism and
storage in the blood bank. Metabolism of cells (RBCs included) can be modeled with genome scale metabolic
models (GEMs) that encapsulate metabolism into a computational form. By using and improving the latest RBC-
GEMs, we aim to achieve translational objectives for transfusion medicine and treating inborn errors of
metabolism.

application_id: 10995987
activity_code: F31
title: Exploring Understudied Proteins to Predict Novel Pathways and Associations to Disease
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Drug discovery has a high rate of failure which affects the number of diseases that are treated. The failure rate is due to the partial knowledge of the functional protein pathways that create the healthy and disease phenotype. Identifying protein pathways is currently a slow, incremental process focusing on a few well-studied proteins, and so this work aims to explore the entire proteome to predict disease mechanisms that can be targeted by drug therapies.

title: Investigating the regulation of lipid homeostasis and lysosomal function in neurodegenerative disease by prosaposin and progranulin in C. elegans
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Perturbation of sphingolipid metabolism, which is regulated by saposins and their precursor protein prosaposin
(PSAP), has recently emerged as a critical risk factor for the onset and progression of neurodegenerative
diseases. The goal of the proposed research is to determine how PSAP cleavage into saposins is regulated by
lysosomal enzymes, progranulin, and age, and how this contributes to lysosomal function. Successful completion
of these experiments will provide novel understanding of the causes and mechanisms underlying lipidome
changes neurodegenerative disease and hopefully bring us one step closer to a cure.
