application_id: 11139585
activity_code: F32
title: Impact of APOE on endothelial cell proteomes in Alzheimer's disease
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is the leading cause of dementia and a growing health concern associated with brain
vascular pathologies. Brain endothelial cells maintain proper structure and function of vasculature, but they
become dysfunctional in AD contributing to cognitive impairment and deposition of pathological proteins in the
brain. This project aims to understand how APOE, the strongest risk gene for developing AD, impacts the protein
changes in endothelial cells in AD individuals and AD mouse model with the ultimate goal to identify new
therapeutic targets and biomarkers for treatment of this devastating disorder.

application_id: 10885930
activity_code: F30
title: Elucidating the Role of Brain Extracellular Matrix in Hippocampal Learning and Memory
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Remodeling of the brain extracellular matrix (ECM) is a medium for synapse plasticity. However, how ECM production contributes to mammalian learning and memory remains unknown. We observed that depletion of aggrecan, a predominant hippocampal extracellular proteoglycan, from dentate gyrus neurons resulted in a deficit in pattern separation, a key hippocampal memory function. This proposal will build off these preliminary data to dissect how learning-induced aggrecan production influences ensemble properties to stabilize hippocampal memories.

title: Comparison of direct and indirect magnetic resonance imaging of myelin in Alzheimer's disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The goal of this project is to compare and evaluate the 3D STAIR-UTE direct myelin imaging technique and the SyMRI indirect myelin imaging technique for evaluation of myelin damage in AD. title: Harnessing Redox Regulation of Proteasome Function to Promote Healthy Aging
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH

title: Research training in Pediatric Endocrinology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
Pediatric Endocrinology is one of the smallest pediatric subspecialties. Obesity and diabetes
(both type 1 and type 2) in youth are on the rise with major current and future impact on overall

title: Investigating the Role of Heme in Acute and Chronic Sickle Cell Disease Pain
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: Project Narrative: While heme in red blood cells is essential for transporting oxygen in the bloodstream, freely circulating heme is a cytotoxic threat that drives systemic inflammation and organ failure in sickle cell disease. This proposal tests the hypothesis that heme is also an essential driver of stimulus-evoked and ongoing, non-evoked pain in sickle cell disease. Understanding the role of elevated heme in the maintenance of pain behavior and sensory neuronal hyperexcitability will aid in the development of novel therapies that reduce the immense burden of SCD pain without the punishing side effects of opioids and neuropathic pain medications.

activity_code: F32
title: Mitochondria-derived dsRNA in brain aging and Alzheimer's disease
org_name: COLORADO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mitochondrial dysfunction and neuroinflammation are major hallmarks of brain aging and age-related neurodegenerative diseases, such as Alzheimer’s disease, and although abnormal mitochondrial function appears to precede inflammation, it remains unclear if or how mitochondrial dysfunction initiates inflammation in brain aging and Alzheimer's disease. This project will explore the potential role of mitochondria-derived double-stranded RNA, which may be released due to mitochondrial dysfunction, in activating inflammatory signaling as an early driver of age/Alzheimer’s disease-related neuroinflammation. The results may provide insight into the biology of brain aging and the development of Alzheimer’s disease.

activity_code: F30
title: Investigating Recruited Lung Macrophage Programming and Turnover in Self-Limited Versus Prolonged Lung Inflammation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) is a common disease with high morbidity and mortality, and the mechanisms which result in some patients recovering rapidly while others experience prolonged inflammation and/or fibrosis are unclear. Pulmonary macrophages play critical roles in the pathogenesis of ARDS by releasing pro-inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, and cytotoxic factors. This proposal will use an existing mouse single-cell RNA sequencing dataset combined with a compartment and lineage tracing mouse model to test the hypothesis that recruited monocyte-derived macrophages drive prolonged inflammation through expression of pro-inflammatory genes and through their continual recruitment to the airspace and pulmonary interstitium.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The exonuclease TREX1 plays a key role in antitumor immune surveillance and autoinflammatory disease by digesting cytosolic DNA to suppress innate immunity. application_id: 10898277
activity_code: F31
title: Determining the nigrostriatal circuit basis of false auditory perceptions.
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative Deficits in sensory processing are common in brain disorders, but the neural substrates of these deficits are poorly understood, and the treatments for them are limited. Evidence suggests that higher-order auditory processing is subject to modulation by dopamine signaling in the striatum—a process that may go awry in diseases like schizophrenia. The current study will combine genetic circuit manipulations with in vivo imaging and behavior to link activity in specific dopamine projections and the modulation of specific striatal neuron types to auditory perception deficits that may be relevant to psychiatric disease.

application_id: 10995420
activity_code: F31
title: Role of Extracellular Long-Chain Acyl-carnitines in the Normal and Stressed Heart
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Recently, we have demonstrated that patients with heart failure have decreased acyl-carnitines in heart biopsies as compared to controls, but still have normal (HFpEF) or increased (HFrEF) acyl-carnitines in the blood. This project tests the hypothesis that extracellular acyl-carnitines are taken up and oxidized by cardiomyocytes and intact hearts, that this rises under hemodynamic or metabolic stress, and is impaired by reduced transporters in HF. Understanding how extracellular acyl-carnitines are (or are not) utilized in the normal heart, and hearts under acute or chronic stress could lead to better strategies to metabolically counter heart failure.

title: Functional analysis of a novel cell envelope integrity protein in Brucella ovis
org_name: MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Bacteria are protected from the surrounding environment by a multi-layer structure known as the cell envelope.
In this study, I will characterize how a recently identified, conserved, and essential protein maintains cell
envelope integrity in the animal pathogen Brucella ovis. This work will provide new understanding of how the
Alphaproteobacteria, which includes the genus Brucella, sustain cellular integrity in the face of inhospitable
environmental conditions.

mission, this PREM establishes a patient-centered quality indicator for palliative care delivery that also

activity_code: K23
title: Harnessing the Power of Technology to Transform Delirium Severity Measurement in the ICU
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Severe delirium, a type of acute confusion often experienced by older adults, leads to worsening Alzheimer’s
Disease and related Dementias and quality of life. Healthcare clinicians need a more timely and
comprehensive measurement of severe delirium in critically ill older adults. Therefore, the current study aims to
develop a passive digital marker for measuring delirium severity that relies on routinely collected data and then
tests if this passive digital marker is usable and acceptable by frontline healthcare clinicians.

In this study, I will characterize how a recently identified, conserved, and essential protein maintains cell envelope integrity in the animal pathogen Brucella ovis. This work will provide new understanding of how the Alphaproteobacteria, which includes the genus Brucella, sustain cellular integrity in the face of inhospitable environmental conditions.

application_id: 10907482
activity_code: F32
title: Regulation of the intestinal colonization niche by epithelial cell death
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative The mechanisms by which the gastrointestinal microbiota influences host immunity remain poorly characterized. The intestinal epithelial cells integrate information from the microbiota, and relay these signals to the downstream immune system, therefore playing a key role as intermediaries in the effect of the microbiota on the entire body. In this project, we propose to study how the microbiota regulates intestinal epithelial cell death and how this can influence disease susceptibility and infectious disease outcomes with the goal of furthering our understanding of epithelial cell function, which will aid the design of new therapies to restore host immune function.

title: Understanding and overcoming Plasmodium-specific immune evasion mechanisms
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative Current malaria vaccines are largely ineffective in individuals previously infected with Plasmodium, the causative agent of malaria, compared to Plasmodium-naïve individuals. activity_code: K08
title: The contribution of hypoxia inducible factor-1-dependent glycolysis in lung interstitial macrophages to the pathobiology of schistosomiasis-induced pulmonary hypertension.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative  Schistosomiasis-induced pulmonary hypertension (Sch-PH) is a major cause of pulmonary arterial hypertension worldwide, a group of incurable illness marked by progressive narrowing of the blood vessels in the lungs and eventual death due to right heart failure. Despite the high prevalence of the disease, the limited understanding of how Sch-PH develops precludes the establishment of effective treatment. This proposal aims to examine the potential role of glucose metabolism in generating the pathologic changes of Sch-PH, with the ultimate goal of identifying metabolism-modulating therapeutic strategies.

title: The Role of Host Genetics in Shaping Intestinal Lactobacillus Population
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  A host’s physiology is contingent on the composition of their microbiome. While a major player in shaping the commensal population is the host’s genetics, due to insufficient models, the mechanisms by which this occurs is unclear. application_id: 11136217
activity_code: F32
title: Deep integration of multi-omics and patient health data to clarify risk factors of enterococcal infective endocarditis
org_name: BROAD INSTITUTE, INC.
phr: Project Narrative
Enterococci cause as much as 26% of all cases of infective endocarditis (IE), a disease among the most
challenging to treat with mortality rates often exceeding 30%. To date, there is a dearth of evidence to explain
patient and bacterial factors most important for the transition from enterococcal bacteremia to IE. The
proposed research will use genomics, proteomics, dense and well-integrated patient health data, and follow up
in vitro studies to disentangle factors associated with enterococcal IE to inform diagnostic, therapeutic and
intervention strategies to curb these infections.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Hyperphosphorylation of microtubule (MT) associated protein Tau is tightly correlated with Alzheimer’s disease progression; however, studies of isoform-specific effects of Tau function and dysfunction are largely unexplored. I discovered that phosphorylation differentially regulates adult and fetal Tau isoforms: Tau isoforms have distinct phosphorylation patterns in neurons, and specific phosphorylation sites impact Tau-MT interactions in an isoform-dependent manner. title: Genetic and Molecular Mechanisms of hypermobile Ehlers Danlos Syndrome
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: Project Narrative Hypermobile Ehlers Danlos Syndrome (hEDS) is a genetic disorder that causes symptoms affecting multiple body systems including the joints, skin, gastrointestinal tract, and heart. Despite its prevalence of 1 in 500 individuals, there are currently no reliable tests or treatments available for hEDS. The completion of this proposal will provide new understanding of the disease, opening new avenues for future studies and the potential for therapeutic approaches that will make a significant impact on the lives of those affected by hEDS and their families.

application_id: 11250129
activity_code: F32
title: Dissecting the transcription factor networks controlling fetal T-lineage differentiation
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
The first wave of thymocyte differentiation in the fetal thymus proceeds rapidly and gives rise to specific
lineages of innate lymphocytes and innate-like T cells that are not generated by any subsequent wave of
development. Based on my preliminary data, I hypothesize that these populations arise from a dedicated
precursor cell in the fetal thymus that is using a unique differentiation program. In this proposal, I aim to test
test this hypothesis through fate mapping and perturbation studies to construct a full lineage hierarchy of fetal
thymocyte differentiation and the gene regulatory networks that govern it.

phr: PROGRAM NARRATIVE There is a pressing need for a diverse workforce of physician scientists who have the skillsets to conduct rigorous interdisciplinary scientific research and develop novel approaches for improving diagnosis and treatment of human disease. To this end, the Tulane StARR program will provide a mentored research and career development program for medicine and pediatric residents to increase the number and diversity of highly trained physician scientists in heart, lung, and blood diseases research. The program engages outstanding preceptors with established track records of mentoring and conducting highly meritorious research, and will foster the next generation of physician scientists able to make discoveries and translate discoveries into improved human health.

application_id: 11092175
activity_code: R25
title: Evidence-based Nutrition Education and Research Guidance (ENER-G)
org_name: NATIONAL UNIVERSITY OF NATURAL MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
The objective of this program is to implement, evaluate, and disseminate rigorous, nutrition
focused, evidence-based nutrition research skills training and mentored research experiences
for trainees and health professionals in both conventional and integrative nutritional science.
While numerous successful models of evidence-based health care skills training exist, these
programs are not sufficient for nutrition trainees who conduct systematic reviews and who apply
nutrition in clinical practice. Evidence-based Nutrition & Research Guidance (ENER-G) will lead
to robust research and clinical practice skills across the Evidence Lifecycle that supports
improvement in the management of diabetes, obesity, and digestive diseases.

application_id: 11118661
activity_code: F32
title: "Evaluating the role of monocytes in the development of an effective HIV vaccine response"
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative:
With an estimated 1.5 million people newly infected with HIV in 2021 alone, HIV remains a critical global health
threat and prevention of acquisition through vaccine development remains a priority. This project aims to
characterize the role of monocytes in the development of HIV vaccine response using a systems biology
approach, for use in future vaccine development. The training plan will provide me with the knowledge and
experience to transition to an independently funded physician scientist working in systems biology and HIV
vaccine development.

application_id: 11093548
activity_code: T32
title: Methods and Logic in Molecular Biology Training Program
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: The Methods and Logic in Molecular Biology (MLMB) Training Program at Princeton University is a multidisciplinary program that trains graduate students to become leaders in biomedical research, teaching, and related life science fields. We use a customized mentoring program that provides MLMB trainees with advanced skills in multidisciplinary research, including critical thinking, generation of hypotheses, experimental design, data analysis and interpretation, and science communication, in conjunction with skills in leadership and mentoring. Research projects directed by MLMB program faculty train Ph.D. candidates in multiple areas related to human health, including reproduction and development, infectious disease, cancer, and aging.

application_id: 11162237
activity_code: F32
title: Interrogating the molecular architecture of the Cryptosporidium host-parasite interface
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Diarrheal disease caused by the parasite Cryptosporidium causes significant death and disability mainly in young
children and immune compromised adults. This single-celled parasite takes up residence in and manipulates
cells of the intestine to extract nutrients and avoid destruction by the immune system. Our work will reveal the
molecular machines used by Cryptosporidium to accomplish these feats, which will help to identify suitable
targets for the development of life-saving vaccines and treatments for this disease.

application_id: 11248720
activity_code: F32
title: Defining the role of PSTPIP2 in neutrophil biology
org_name: CEDARS-SINAI MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Understanding neutrophil development and the regulation of their effector functions is essential for advancing
treatments of various infectious and sterile inflammatory diseases. The goal of this proposal is to gain
comprehensive insights into the functional significance of PSTPIP2 in neutrophil biology. The outcomes of this
research will advance our understanding of the innate immune regulations by defining the role of PSTPIP2 in
granulopoiesis and also determine its contribution to neutrophilic defenses against Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

application_id: 11118659
activity_code: F32
title: Proteus mirabilis RTX proteins: Role in polymicrobial biofilm formation and pathogenesis of catheter associated urinary tract infection
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: Project Narrative
Catheter-associated urinary tract infections (CAUTI) are the most common nosocomial infections, with the
majority being polymicrobial in nature. Proteus mirabilis is one of the most common causes of CAUTIs and it
is frequently found to be co-colonizing with Enterococcus faecalis, with patients colonized by both pathogens
suffering a significant increase in disease severity and risk of developing secondary sequelae, such as
bacteremia. Understanding how these two disease-enhancing pathogens interact, defining the important factors
and their role in biofilm formation, persistence, and pathogenesis of polymicrobial CAUTI has the potential to
drive novel therapeutic development and inform best medical intervention practices.

title: Research Training of Anesthesiology Physician-Scientists
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: Narrative
The proposed program will provide support for postgraduate research training in the Department
of Anesthesiology at McGovern Medical School as part of the University of Texas Health Science
Center at Houston to qualified applicants who have committed to or are enrolled in postgraduate
training in anesthesiology. The goal of this training is to provide the next generation of physician-
scientists and researchers with the tools and training necessary for those who seek to improve
medical care in areas relevant to perioperative care, palliative care, acute and chronic pain and
health services delivery.

application_id: 11237957
activity_code: F32
title: CXCL13 as an aging-associated defect in memory T cell homeostasis
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
79% of flu-related deaths between the 2010/11-2019/20 seasons were of people 65 years or older, and
although vaccinations are strongly encouraged in this group, elderly vaccine responses are consistently
impaired. This study will define the role of CXCL13 as an aging-associated defect in memory T cell
homeostasis using CRISPR-engineered tonsil organoids. Results obtained here will improve our understanding
of aging immune memory, thus providing the potential for future therapies directed at this axis and limiting the
number of deaths to vaccine-preventable diseases.

application_id: 10785483
activity_code: K99
title: Deciphering the role of mitochondrial metabolism in liver cancer
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: Current treatment options for liver cancer, the third leading cause of cancer-related death, provide only limited survival benefits, in part due to challenges in targeting genetically distinct liver tumors. With this proposal, as a potential therapeutic strategy, we will identify and target the metabolic changes imposed by oncogenic alterations on cancer cell mitochondria, the central biosynthetic hub for cellular metabolism. Combining genetic in vivo liver tumorigenesis models, mitochondrial metabolomics and CRISPR-based functional screens, this proposal will assess oncogene-dependent metabolic limitations of tumor growth in vivo and, provide unprecedented opportunities to exploit metabolic intervention strategies targeting liver cancer.

application_id: 10994911
activity_code: F32
title: Elucidating the role of Akkermansia muciniphila in Group B Streptococcal vaginal colonization and ascending infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Group B Streptococcus (GBS), a frequent colonizer of the vaginal tract, is also a leading cause of neonatal sepsis and meningitis and can be transmitted to a newborn during vaginal delivery or by ascension into the uterus during pregnancy. We have found that GBS is associated with the Gram-negative mucin-degrading obligate anaerobe Akkermansia muciniphila, a novel vaginal commensal, and in pregnant females the presence of both GBS and A. muciniphila coincide with pre-term birth. This proposal aims to characterize the properties of A. muciniphila that contribute to GBS persistence and how mucin-degradation alters the vaginal environment to promote GBS ascension.

application_id: 11179108
activity_code: F32
title: Understanding the role of transmembrane proteins for Plasmodium cell division
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The malaria parasite causes a staggering global health burden, yet its key biological structures and processes
remain understudied. To better understand parasite cell division, this proposal focuses on the proteins that
comprise the cytokinesis-driving contractile ring. By investigating the role of three transmembrane proteins, the
goal is to reveal novel insights into parasite-specific mechanisms of cytokinesis that can inform the next
generation of antimalarial drugs.

application_id: 11176697
activity_code: F32
title: Synaptopodin and its role in Shigella flexneri intercellular spread
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative
Shigella flexneri is a major source of diarrheal illness with severity of illness being directly related to the ability
of S. flexneri to spread intercellularly between monolayers of intestinal epithelial cells within the colon.
Whereas the major steps of intercellular spread are known, the bacterial-host interactions that occur, which allow
S. flexneri intracellular passage from one cell to an adjacent cell are not well understood. This proposal aims to
define the molecular mechanisms for how Synaptopodin promotes S. flexneri intercellular spread thereby
increasing severity of diarrheal illness.

application_id: 10885958
activity_code: K23
title: Reversibility of brain glucose kinetics in type 2 diabetic subjects
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: Chronic hyperglycemia has been associated with a decrease in brain glucose transport through down-regulation of GLUT1 at the blood brain barrier, findings that could be explained as a protective adaptation of the central nervous system against the harmful consequences of hyperglycemia. However, GLUT1 down regulation may lead to reduced brain glucose metabolism leading to neuronal damage, neurodegeneration and cognitive decline. This application will determine whether improvement of glycemic control in poorly controlled type 2 diabetic subjects would restore glucose brain transport kinetics by using classic metabolic studies methodology as well as state-of-the-art brain nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) techniques.

activity_code: F32
title: Synaptopodin and its role in Shigella flexneri intercellular spread
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative Shigella flexneri is a major source of diarrheal illness with severity of illness being directly related to the ability of S. flexneri to spread intercellularly between monolayers of intestinal epithelial cells within the colon. Whereas the major steps of intercellular spread are known, the bacterial-host interactions that occur, which allow S. flexneri intracellular passage from one cell to an adjacent cell are not well understood. This proposal aims to define the molecular mechanisms for how Synaptopodin promotes S. flexneri intercellular spread thereby increasing severity of diarrheal illness.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The Neuroscience Colorado Research Experience (N-CORE) is an innovative summer research experience for undergraduate students from underrepresented backgrounds in the Denver Metropolitan and Regional area. N-CORE fosters a community for like-minded students from similar backgrounds—a key component for retention of URM students in biomedical fields.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The healing of large bone defects, greater than the intrinsic capacity of self-regeneration, remains a significant
clinical challenge with approximately 500,000 patients undergoing bone transplants each year in the US alone.
Natural fracture healing involves a coordinated series of biological events, including inflammation,
vascularization, and osteogenesis, precisely orchestrated in time and space, to repair and regenerate bone
tissue. The proposed research aims to develop an innovative biomaterial approach in which granular hydrogels
are used to manipulate the regenerative secretome, specifically extracellular vesicles (EVs), of bone marrow-
derived mesenchymal stem/stromal cells and then subsequently engineered to serve as delivery vehicles for

application_id: 10996074
activity_code: F31
title: Defining the role of persistent DNA bridges in tumor-intrinsic immune activation in hereditary breast and ovarian cancer
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Persistent DNA bridges are manifestations of genomic instability, a hallmark of cancer, and arise with replication stress, telomere dysfunction, and errors in double-strand break repair. My proposed studies will elucidate the mechanisms of innate immune activation by persistent DNA bridges arising from PARP inhibitor treatment in DNA repair-deficient hereditary breast and ovarian cancer. These studies could lead to the development of potential predictive biomarkers of PARP inhibitor response, as well as novel insights that can maximize the therapeutic potential of PARP inhibitor treatment in DNA repair-deficient tumors.

application_id: 10996431
activity_code: F31
title: Exploring synaptic actin-myosin dynamics with super resolution microscopy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative Nonmuscle Myosin IIB (NMIIB) is an ATPase motor protein that generates force on actin filaments to serve as an essential driver of the actin cytoskeleton’s re-organization during synaptic plasticity. NMIIB activity is necessary for this spine structural plasticity that contributes to learning and memory storage, yet it is poorly understood. The development of a knock-in, endogenously tagged NMIIB mouse line will allow for super resolution microscopy studies to uncover the localization of NMIIB throughout the neuron at rest and during neuronal stimulation and then to measure the subcellar dynamics of NMIIB during stimulation and visualize the effect of NMIIB activity on actin dynamics.

application_id: 10997211
activity_code: F32
title: Multiscale mechano-metabolic regulation of fibrous tissue homeostasis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Meniscus tears are among the most common knee injuries and lead to osteoarthritis, especially in patients with metabolic risk factors like obesity. The goal of this project is to elucidate how signals from adipose tissue influence meniscus cell response to homeostatic and injury-associated mechanical cues. Our results will establish the basis for mechanical-metabolic interactions in meniscus health and degeneration, providing insight towards novel meniscus repair strategies in high-risk patients.

application_id: 10898151
activity_code: F30
title: Follicle-Inherent Biomechanical Mechanisms Underlying Ovulation
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed studies will provide novel insight into the biomechanical forces and regulatory processes that drive ovulation while simultaneously providing me with scientific and leadership skills to be a successful clinician- scientist. I propose that during ovulation, progesterone decreases expression of a calcium channel, TRPV4, which promotes fluid accumulation in the ovarian follicle and leads to a disproportionate increase in pressure on the region of the follicle wall that will rupture to release the cumulus oocyte complex. Quantifying the biomechanical forces and regulators that drive follicle rupture during ovulation will lead to improved understanding of anovulatory infertility and to the development of novel non-hormonal contraceptives.

title: Investigating how chemotherapeutic thiopurines inhibit telomerase elongation of telomeres
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Telomerase is a prime target for cancer therapeutics since it is expressed in over 85% of cancers and enables unlimited cell division, but is lacking in most non-cancerous cells. This fellowship will investigate how the chemotherapeutic thiopurine, 6-thio-dG, inhibits telomerase’s ability to restore telomeres, causing telomere shortening and cellular growth arrest, and whether inhibiting thiopurine sanitase can improve 6-thio-dG efficacy. This study will provide mechanistic insight on how 6-thio-dG inhibits telomerase and halts cancer cell proliferation, which can inform future basic and clinical research studies.

application_id: 11180056
activity_code: F32
title: What is the mechanism by which a gene essential for Rhabdoid tumor viability regulates Chromatin function?
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Mutation of genes involved in chromatin regulation frequently underlie cancer. We have identified a gene
whose deletion causes cell death specifically in childhood cancers that are mutant for the SWI/SNF chromatin
remodeler, in which the antagonistic Polycomb complex gains control. The proposed studies are designed to
determine the normal function of this gene and how its deletion kills these childhood cancers, with the goal of
improving our mechanistic understanding of the interplay between SWI/SNF and Polycomb complexes in
cancer control.

application_id: 10996049
activity_code: F31
title: Lifespace mobility and cognitive decline after stroke
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Stroke is the third leading cause of disability globally, and the number of incident strokes is expected to increase substantially as the size of the population aged 65 and older grows. After a stroke, some patients experience an accelerated decline in cognitive and functional ability, while disability stabilizes for other patients. We will leverage two studies to evaluate the relationship between life-space and long-term cognitive decline after stroke.

application_id: 10994351
activity_code: F32
title: A Hyper-Thrombotic State and Immunosuppression in GBM
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: Project Narrative Cancer-related thrombosis is the second leading cause of death for cancer patients and glioblastoma (GBM), the most common primary malignant brain tumor, is associated with approximately a 30% risk of venous thromboembolism, deep vein thrombosis, or pulmonary embolisms. While this remains a major clinical issue, the role that the hyper-thrombotic state plays on contributing to the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment in GBM has not been explored. This study aims to understand how platelet hyperactivity and the thrombin-PAR4 signaling axis contributes to the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment in a sex-dependent manner.

application_id: 10826789
activity_code: F32
title: Engineering RIG-I Activating Nanoparticles for Glioblastoma Immunotherapy
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: Glioblastoma is a form of brain cancer with poor treatment options, an average survival time of 12-18 months post-diagnosis, and a five-year survival rate of less than 10%. Immunotherapy has shown promise for other cancer types, and we propose to design and test an innovative new immune activating RNA therapy for glioblastoma. We hypothesize that our therapy will generate a robust anti-tumor response without damaging the surrounding brain tissue.

application_id: 11001199
activity_code: K23
title: Understanding longitudinal microbiome change in relation to immunologic outcomes in pediatric SCT recipients.
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Allogeneic stem cell transplant (SCT) is a life-saving treatment for children with leukemia, immunodeficiencies, and other disorders of the hematopoietic system, yet its success is limited by aberrant or delayed development of the new immune system. The gastrointestinal tract microbiota, and microbiome disruption that occurs over the course of SCT, may predict or even contribute to the pathogenesis of these immune-related outcomes, including graft-versus-host disease and time to immune cell recovery. This study will further define the relationship between microbiome disruption and immunologic outcomes of SCT, laying the foundation to develop new biomarkers and therapeutic approaches to improve the success of this treatment.

application_id: 11141554
activity_code: F32
title: Optimizing treatment decisions and quality-adjusted life years through improved lung cancer staging
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Appropriate treatment of non-small cell lung cancer is predicated on accurate characterization of cancer
spread to lymph nodes, which can occur via endobronchial ultrasound guided transbronchial needle aspiration
or surgical mediastinoscopy. While both procedures can confirm the presence of cancer in lymph nodes, their
ability to identify the extent of cancer spread has never been compared but has the potential to change
treatment decisions and consequently impact quality-adjusted life years. Findings from this study will 1)
determine if one procedure is superior to another, 2) examine optimal methods of performing lymph node
biopsies, and 3) minimize diagnostic errors, optimize treatment selection, and improve patient outcomes.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEBRASKA MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative:
This training program in the fundamentals of biochemistry and molecular biology is designed to equip the
next generation of scientists with the skills and basic knowledge that provide the foundation for all modern
biomedical science. Key to our mission is to enhance the diversity of our trainees by recruiting and
retaining a diverse student body. Students who graduate from this program will be trained in critical
thinking, ethical and independent research, and a variety of key skills in biochemistry and molecular
biology that will lead to employment in academic and biomedical research in industry, teaching, and a
variety of additional science careers.

application_id: 11166275

title: The frequency and function of Type 3 Innate Lymphoid Cells regulates the melanoma tumor microenvironment
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Treatment of malignant melanoma has been revolutionized with the introduction of immune checkpoint
inhibitors (ICI); however, response to these therapies is not universal. ICI rely on recruitment of T cells into the
tumor microenvironment (TME) to perpetuate an anti-tumor immune response. The role of the innate immune
system in recruitment of T cells into the TME and how this changes during tumor progression is largely
unknown.

title: Training Program in Psychiatric Genetics and Translational Research
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: 8. Narrative
Scientific advances in genetics require complex and novel methods and their application in the
complex setting of psychiatric disorders. To reduce the suffering and disability related to mental
illness in society, clinical research needs to translate findings from genetics and ongoing
developments in the neurosciences into better treatment, prevention, and healthcare strategies.

populations and the impact of health disparities in order to improve population mental health.

application_id: 11141561
activity_code: F32
title: Defining the role of stress granules in cancer initiation
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative.
Stress granules are cytoprotective biological condensates that are induced by cell stress and are hijacked by
cancer cells to withstand the harsh environment of tumorigenesis. This proposal leverages a humanized
zebrafish melanoma model to test the essentiality and epitranscriptomic influence of stress granule formation in
melanoma precursor cells on melanoma initiation and progression in vivo. As stress granules are observed in
many cancer systems, the findings of this proposal will uncover foundational knowledge that can be used to
exploit a conserved molecular pathway of cancer initiating cells, aiding the effort to control cancer in the long-term
by stopping it before it can start.

application_id: 10818535
activity_code: K08
title: Development of strategies to enhance titin (TTN) expression and treat dilated cardiomyopathy caused by TTN haploinsufficiency
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Rare genetic variants of the essential sarcomere protein titin (TTN) is the most common genetic cause of dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM), a disease characterized by enlargement and weakening of one or both of main chambers of the heart. DCM often progresses to heart failure, a devastating disorder with high morbidity and early mortality, but only limited options are currently available to treat DCM. This proposal aims to determine how TTN gene expression is regulated, augment the amount of TTN expression via genome editing, assess effects of increased TTN protein level on cardiomyocytes with pathogenic TTN variants, and ultimately establish therapeutic strategies for DCM.

application_id: 11082365
activity_code: F32
title: Characterizing the connectivity and molecular composition of opioid-sensitive neurons in the periaqueductal gray
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
Opioids are powerful analgesics, but their harmful side effects have led to a nationwide opioid epidemic.
Neurons in the periaqueductal gray (PAG) contribute to the pain-relieving and rewarding effects of opioids, but
the organization of these circuits and how they contribute to opioid-induced behaviors remains unclear. To
elucidate how opioids affect PAG neurons, I will characterize the organization of PAG neural circuits and
investigate how they contribute to opioid-induced behaviors using input-output circuit mapping, single-cell
transcriptomics, and circuit-specific neuromodulation experiments in freely moving animals.

title: Nucleus accumbens dynamics underlying cue-reward learning
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Drug associated cues are powerful drivers of craving and relapse in people with substance use disorder.
Contexts that contain reward-predictive cues often contain many other cues unrelated to reward. This proposal
aims to determine how neuronal activity in the nucleus accumbens contributes to the learning of and response
to selective cue-reward associations in order to better understand the neural basis of healthy and disordered
reward seeking.

title: Macrophage-mediated interleukin-6 signaling drives ryanodine receptor 2 calcium leak in postoperative atrial fibrillation
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There are a half-million open heart surgeries annually in the United States, and roughly 165,000 of these patients will develop postoperative atrial fibrillation (poAF). As the risk of chronic AF is eight-fold greater in poAF patients, developing safe and effective targeted therapies that work within the relevant timeframe would significantly reduce rates of chronic atrial fibrillation, which is the most common arrhythmia worldwide and a leading cause of ischemic stroke that currently has no disease-modifying therapies. Thus, this research is relevant to the NHLBI’s mission as it will enhance the health and lives of the many individuals who undergo cardiothoracic surgery by reducing the risk of poAF as well as future AF and stroke.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
 Very early identification of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) cognitive vulnerability is difficult because the current
gold-standard tests are 1) only sensitive to obvious, not subtle, cognitive impairment, and 2) measure peak
performance at a single time point in a well-controlled environment that is detached from how a person
functions in day-to-day life. Mobile health (mHealth) assessments that utilize common smartphone technology
may help overcome these limitations by sampling cognitive functioning multiple times per day in ‘real-time’,
characterizing not only the time-course of a person’s cognitive performance, but also the psychosocial context
(e.g., interpersonal interactions, mood, loneliness) in which they are functioning. This study will establish
mHealth tools to iden...

application_id: 10887599
activity_code: T32
title: Graduate Training in Nutrition
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: The discipline of nutrition touches on individuals of all nationalities and backgrounds, and indeed the most serious sequela of malnutrition are observed in the poorest and most under-represented sectors of society. In recognition of this fact, the IHN and this Training Program have long believed it was our responsibility not only to advance understanding of the underlying causes and consequences of malnutrition, both under-nutrition and increasingly, over-nutrition, but also to provide training to individuals from the communities most affected by the adverse health effects of poor nutrition. In this light, the IHN has for over 25 years maintained a vigorous PhD Training Program in nutritional sciences, renewal of support for which is sought in this application.

title: Intraindividual Cognitive Variability as an Early Marker of Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative  Very early identification of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) cognitive vulnerability is difficult because the current gold-standard tests are 1) only sensitive to obvious, not subtle, cognitive impairment, and 2) measure peak performance at a single time point in a well-controlled environment that is detached from how a person functions in day-to-day life. Mobile health (mHealth) assessments that utilize common smartphone technology may help overcome these limitations by sampling cognitive functioning multiple times per day in ‘real-time’, characterizing not only the time-course of a person’s cognitive performance, but also the psychosocial context (e.g., interpersonal interactions, mood, loneliness) in which they are functioning. This study will establish mHealth tools to iden...

title: Diabetes Self-Management Education and Support for People with Type 2 Diabetes Experiencing Food Insecurity: A Mixed Methods Study
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Food insecurity disproportionately affects people with diabetes mellitus and is associated with suboptimal diabetes self-management behaviors and poor diabetes outcomes such as hypoglycemia, poor glycemic control, and increased acute healthcare utilization. A diabetes self-management education and support program tailored to the needs of people with food insecurity may improve these outcomes. The proposed research will use quantitative and qualitative methods to determine the nutritional, behavioral, and psychological barriers to diabetes self-management for people with food insecurity and type 2 diabetes to inform the adaptation and development of a DSMES program tailored for this population.

application_id: 11218663
activity_code: F32
title: Cellular Mechanisms of Acute-Intermittent Hypercapnic-Hypoxia Induced Phrenic Motor Plasticity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
My focus concerns plasticity in the neural system controlling breathing, specifically phrenic long-term facilitation
(pLTF) following acute intermittent hypoxia (AIH). I propose to uncover mechanism(s) of heightened pLTF when
comparing acute intermittent hypercapnic-hypoxia (AIHH) versus AIH without hypercapnia by testing the
hypothesis that AIHH enhances plasticity by optimizing the balance between competing pathways to phrenic
motor facilitation. Pursuing this research will train me in new methods and ways of thinking, advance our
understanding of respiratory motor plasticity, and may impact clinical trial design since AIH is being explored as
a treatment to improve respiratory and non-respiratory motor function in people with spinal cord injuries and ALS.

application_id: 11140271
activity_code: F32
title: The Role of Ovarian Hormones in Regulating Dopamine Acetylcholine Interactions in the Nucleus Accumbens
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
Sex differences in nicotine effects combined with blunted responses to treatment approaches combine to lead
to worse treatment outcomes for nicotine addiction in women. Nicotine acts through hijacking endogenous
acetylcholine signaling, which ultimately leads to the increases in dopamine release that drive nicotine
reinforcement. This grant focuses on outlining how ovarian hormones contribute to sex differences in
dopamine release regulation by acetylcholine in female subjects, and will provide a better understanding for
pharmacotherapeutic approaches for nicotine treatment in women.

activity_code: F31
title: Arousal-dependent modulation of layer 5 neurons in the auditory cortex
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Arousal is a ubiquitous influence on the brain that continuously fluctuates during wakefulness and enables sensation and perception. This project aims to elucidate the arousal-dependent effects on subpopulations of excitatory cortical neurons in terms of their response properties and subsequent impact on behavior. Results from these experiments could advance our understanding of the neural correlates of optimal performance and motivate novel theories on decision-making, sensory encoding, and the function of specific cortical cell-types.

title: Neoglycosylation Epitopes in Metaplasia and Cancer

title: Input-specific synaptic plasticity in the nucleus accumbens evoked by inhibition of angiotensin-converting enzyme
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Synaptic plasticity changes in a region of the brain called the nucleus accumbens underlie the formation of substance use disorders. This proposal will investigate how angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibition, which modulates endogenous opioid signaling, affects synaptic plasticity within specific neural inputs to the nucleus accumbens. application_id: 10785887
activity_code: K99
title: Understanding how transposons generate new immune gene protein-coding splice variants
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: Project Narrative More than 80% of human protein coding genes are alternatively spliced, effectively increasing the proteome in comparison to the total number of genes. While transposable elements have been largely recognized to provide alternative promoters and enhancers, their contribution to the protein coding sequences is still debated. The goal of this proposal is to understand how transposable elements shape the evolution and diversification of vertebrate proteomes, as well as which events and mechanisms promote their origin and affect their expression.

application_id: 11158568
activity_code: F32
title: Cannabis use, trauma, and self-regulatory cognitive processes: A multimodal study integrating biobehavioral markers and ecological assessment
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Although cannabis use for both therapeutic and recreational treatment of mental health concerns is increasing,
especially among emerging adults, a research gap assessing the cognitive and psychobiological mechanisms
underlying cannabis use behaviors following a traumatic event remains. Two important cognitive processes
involved in cannabis use decision-making are inhibitory control and reward motivation. The proposed project
utilizes a multi-modal approach (i.e., psychophysiology, behavioral tasks, ecological momentary assessment,
computational modeling) to identify and isolate idiographic profiles of these decisional processes and develop
longitudinal models of posttraumatic stress and cannabis use behavior in emerging adults.

title: Enabling Discovery-Based Brain Metabolomics with Ultra-High Resolution Liquid Chromatography and Machine Learning
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cocaine use disorder is one of the most challenging disorders for society to address due to its rapid progression
and lack of approved treatments/therapies. Identifying the neurochemical changes arising from this chronic
disorder will be beneficial for providing early medical intervention for those affected; however, current technology
is not capable of discovering these chemical alterations. In this project, we will develop new instrumentation and
data analysis techniques that enhance our ability to monitor neurochemicals in the living brain and address
questions relating to the underlying cause of cocaine use disorder within an animal model for addiction.

application_id: 11095596
activity_code: T32
title: Multidisciplinary Research Training in Palliative Care
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative
Despite advances in clinical care and education, gaps remain in the evidence base to inform clinical palliative
care, especially for older adults. One of the most important ways to advance care for older adults with advanced
illness is through the development of early-stage scientists who will contribute to generation of evidence and
build the academic critical mass necessary for the future of the field. Although clinical fellowship programs in
palliative care are established, there remains a paucity of research training opportunities in palliative care,
therefore the goal of this T32 is to prepare postdoctoral physician and PhD scientists for careers as aging-related
palliative care researchers.

title: Center for Underrepresented Research in Addiction (CURA)
org_name: LOYOLA UNIVERSITY CHICAGO
phr: Lay Abstract The number of African Americans, Hispanic Americans, American Indians, and Pacific Islanders who do drug addiction research is much lower than would be expected based on their representation in the U.S. population. We will establish a national Center for Underrepresented Research in Addiction (CURA) program that will promote the advancement of these underrepresented minorities who are junior faculty members in academia. The program will involve a series of workshops and mentoring over several years with instruction and mentoring in drug addiction research, professional development, and ethics provided by a large number of nationally recognized faculty members.

application_id: 11262179
activity_code: F32
title: Investigating the role of dorsal raphe nucleus enkephalin neurons in motivational and affective behaviors during chronic pain
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Chronic pain affects more than 30% of the adult population and often leads to comorbidities such as depression,
anxiety and increased risk of suicide. It is now appreciated that chronic pain is associated with dysregulation of
affective and motivational circuits in the brain, both of which are highly sensitive to endogenous opioid signaling.
Here, we seek to leverage next-generation, opioid specific technologies to determine the biological and
computational function of endogenous opioids within the dorsal raphe nucleus in regulating chronic pain-induced
dysregulation of motivated behaviors.

title: Authentic Community Engagement in Science (ACES)
org_name: MONTANA STATE UNIVERSITY -BILLINGS
phr: Project Narrative
The Atomic Circus Experience (ACE) Project has the goal of exposing students and the
communities of Eastern Montana to STEM and biomedical concepts and careers, helping them
recognize and understand the pathways to future biomedical employment. The ACE goal is
accomplished through collaborations with local educators, biomedical professionals, and
community members to develop a customized multimedia biochemistry stage show, as well as
culturally relevant, place-based STEM curriculum. The curriculum engages students with local
community members involved in biomedical and STEM work, the show ignites a passion for
learning which then paves the way for solving local issues using biochemical and STEM skills
and knowledge.

application_id: 10901361
activity_code: F32
title: Patient-Reported Benefits of Cochlear Implant Listening Configurations
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Currently, the decision to continue using bimodal hearing or pursue a second cochlear implant (i.e., transition from bimodal hearing to bilateral cochlear implant use) is highly subjective, and no clinical guidelines exist to support this decision. Assessing the patient-reported benefits of each listening configuration has been understudied; however, previous literature and our preliminary data suggest that patients hold valuable insights into real-world listening benefits that may differentiate bimodal hearing and bilateral cochlear implants. In this study, we will investigate the patient-reported advantages and limitations of each listening configuration using a novel research approach.

org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: Project Narrative Sarcoidosis is a multisystem inflammatory disease that is known to cause disproportionate morbidity to Black patients, and no interventions have been developed or tested to address this known disparity. application_id: 11120967
activity_code: R25
title: Biotechnology Incubator for Teachers (BRITE)
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: BRITE Project Narrative
We propose the Biotechnology Research Incubator for Teachers (BRITE) program to provide
middle school teachers in the Houston area with summer research opportunities, aligning with
Next Generation Science Standards and bridging the gap between research experiences, AI-
guided literature adaptation, and classroom instruction. With a cohort of 12 teachers in the first
year and 16 teachers annually in subsequent years, BRITE aims to enhance science education,
empower teachers, and inspire students to pursue STEMM fields, impacting approximately
7,000 middle school students over five years. The program's influence will extend further as
trained teachers disseminate knowledge and influence their colleagues, amplifying its reach.

application_id: 10994126
activity_code: F32
title: Modeling the effects of binaural midbrain coding and efferent gain control on the perception of complex sounds
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: The proposed research applies computational models of the human auditory system to better understand how listeners can detect sounds of interest against a background of competing sounds, such as a target talker speaking in a noisy room. The proposed models can simulate the effects of hearing loss and will be used to investigate how hearing loss at the peripheral level impacts neural coding of sound at higher levels of the auditory system, such as the inferior colliculus in the auditory midbrain. The results of this research will inform strategies to ameliorate perceptual changes associated with hearing loss in next-generation hearing aids and cochlear implants.

application_id: 11188958
activity_code: F32
title: Language plasticity after childhood hemispherectomy: Neuroimaging and computational modeling
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This research aims to characterize how the functional map of the left or right cerebral hemisphere can change
to accommodate a cognitive function that is ordinarily lateralized to the opposite cerebral hemisphere, when the
opposite cerebral hemisphere is damaged early in life. This investigation is especially important for language
processing, which is typically supported in the left hemisphere and is resistant to reorganizing into the right
hemisphere after left hemisphere injury in adulthood. By examining how functions can reorganize after the onset
of seizures and then the surgical removal of part of one cerebral hemisphere during childhood, we can advance
fundamental knowledge about how cognitive functions become mapped to cortical regions, and how this
mapping can be...

application_id: 11095037
activity_code: T32
title: PIGE: The Program Integrating Genomics and the Environment
org_name: WAYNE STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: The goal of the Program Integrating Genomics and Exposures (PIGE) between Wayne State University (WSU) and Case Western Reserve University (CWRU) is to bridge the disciplines of exposure science and environmental epidemiology/genomics, two scientific areas with significant impact on public health, by providing a training program to develop the next generation of scientific researchers. Currently, scientifically disciplines, particularly exposure science and genetic epidemiology/genomics, are primarily performed in silos where integration is limited thus delaying important advances. Here, we build on several WSU and CWRU collaborations to create the PIGE where the next generation of scientists with integrated expertise in both
environmental science and genomics.

application_id: 11011267
activity_code: F31
title: Artificial Metalloenzymes Containing Uranyl Cofactors for Photocatalytic C-H Bond Functionalization
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project Narrative: This project aims to develop a new methodology for the site- and stereoselective formation of C–C bond from C–H bonds through the use of a photocatalytically active uranyl-containing artificial metalloenzyme. Through directed evolution using techniques such as site-saturation mutagenesis, artificial metalloenzymes containing non-native, uranyl cofactors will be developed to catalyze asymmetric chemical reactions with high activity and high selectivity. This will create new approaches by which molecules relevant to human health can be synthesized by new classes of selective C–H bond functionalization.

application_id: 11031405
activity_code: K23
title: Odor memory and functional neuroimaging in cognitively impaired older adults and Alzheimer's disease
org_name: NEW YORK STATE PSYCHIATRIC INSTITUTE DBA RESEARCH FOUNDATION FOR MENTAL HYGIENE, INC
phr: Project Narrative
As the world’s population continues to age, the prevalence of Alzheimer’s disease will increase, generating
greater urgency for creating methods capable of identifying individuals at risk for decline. This project will
facilitate development of tools for enhancing early detection of Alzheimer’s disease, which in turn will allow for
earlier intervention and improved methods to screen patients for clinical trials. Results will provide clinically
applicable information on how olfactory deficit patterns manifest across early stages of the disease, and can
potentially be used for improving diagnostic accuracy compared to normal aging, which in turn can reduce time
spent in workups and can reduce delays in initiating treatment.

activity_code: K23
title: Improving medication adherence and disease control for patients with multimorbidity: the role of price transparency tools
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Many middle-aged and older adults who live with multimorbidity report abandoning their medications or skipping doses because of cost. Patients are unable to account for medication costs when making medical decisions, because they rarely know what those costs will be before getting to the pharmacy. Under the guidance of her mentors, Dr. Sloan will evaluate the uptake and acceptability of a novel clinician-facing out-of-pocket price transparency tool, as well as its impact on clinical outcomes and adherence among middle-aged and older adults with multimorbidity.

application_id: 10866614
activity_code: K23
title: Development of Emergent PET Tracers in Frontotemporal Lobar Degeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) is a leading cause of dementia and one of the most common causes of early- onset dementia. Diverse molecular pathologies cause FTD (collectively known as frontotemporal lobar degeneration [FTLD] pathology), which has complicated the development of positron emission tomography (PET) tracers for these diseases. This project will evaluate and validate new PET tracers, which could improve FTD/FTLD diagnosis and differential diagnosis and facilitate drug development for these diseases.

title: PARTNERS: Program to Advance Research and Transdisciplinary TraiNing in the CarE of PeRsons with Down Syndrome
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: NARRATIVE A major obstacle to addressing gaps in our understanding of Down Syndrome [DS] and its related

application_id: 10995425
activity_code: F32
title: The impact of noise on temporal integration of speech in the human brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed research will reveal how the brain integrates information about speech over time, both in silence and in the presence of background noise. The results of these experiments will further our knowledge of the computations that enable speech recognition in noisy real-world conditions, as well as how auditory cortex flexibly adapts to changing environments. Answering these questions is a critical step towards understanding the deficits in listening in noise that are present in auditory neurodevelopmental and attentional disorders.

application_id: 10906161
activity_code: F32
title: Dearomatization of Planar Aromatic Cores into Three-Dimensional Scaffolds with Defined Stereochemistry
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative Naphthalenones are bicyclic scaffolds found in many natural and synthetic compounds which display a diverse range of biological activities including anti-viral, anti-bacterial, and anti-fungal properties. Through protein engi- neering and directed evolution, I propose to generate enzymes capable of catalyzing dearomatization of planar naphthols into three-dimensional naphthalenone scaffolds via nitrene or carbene insertions. This work will open new avenues of enzyme catalyzed nitrene or carbene chemistries, as well as provide a facile and sustainable method for transforming readily available aromatic feedstocks into intricate pharmacophores.

org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: The complexity of neuroscience data and the challenges of understanding and treating diseases and dysfunctions of the brain demand an unprecedented level of sophistication in data analysis and modeling. application_id: 11301802
activity_code: K23
title: Cerebral Autoregulation, Metabolic derangement, and Edema in Encephalopathy Outcome (CAMEEO)
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Episodes of altered mental status, from slight confusion to delirium to coma, are a leading complication of
acute medical illness in hospitalized patients and are associated with increased risk of death, long-term
cognitive impairment, and increased risk of vascular dementia and Alzheimer’s dementia. This study seeks to
improve understanding of the mechanisms that govern the brain’s pathologic response (encephalopathy and
delirium) to the metabolic derangements during acute medical illness and how those mechanisms influence
cognitive impairment and subsequently increased risk for developing early vascular dementia, Alzheimer’s
dementia, or other related dementias after acute medical illness.

activity_code: F31
title: Transition and Health Implications of Posttraumatic Growth in Pediatric Cancer Survivors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  While there are over 483,000 childhood cancer survivors in the United States and that number is increasing, only about a third of these individuals receive age-appropriate survivorship care. Without adequate psychosocial support, symptoms of posttraumatic stress may increase risk of not receiving guideline-based follow-up care, especially among young adults who are already at risk for getting "lost in transition" from pediatric to adult health care. To address calls from the NCI, this study seeks to target survivorship transition and healthy lifestyle engagement in survivors by investigating posttraumatic growth (PTG) as a potential modifiable factor that may be linked to positive health outcomes among pediatric cancer survivors.

application_id: 10807716
activity_code: K23
title: Impact of Hourly Neurochecks in Critically Ill Older Adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative Frequent brain examinations are an important tool for monitoring patients after brain injury, but if excessive, may cause sleep disruption and worsened outcomes in hospitalized patients. Interrupting sleep may make people confused (delirious) and may ultimately increase risk for dementia similar to Alzheimer’s disease. Improving patients’ sleep in the intensive care unit may improve their outcomes, so this project studies whether checking brain status hourly versus every-other-hour impacts sleep quantity and short or long-term cognitive outcomes.

title: Biofabrication Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Biofabrication merges materials science processing with controlled cellular behavior, with impacts on fields such
as tissue engineering and regenerative medicine, drug delivery, three-dimensional cell culture, and in vitro tissue
models. To further impact these fields and to educate a future workforce, new training programs are needed that
not only educate in quantitative fundamentals and biofabrication techniques, but also drive the intersection of
basic research and commercialization. Through our innovative training program, including a biofabrication
course, industry exposure, co-mentorship to harness the expertise of Preceptors, and a bi-weekly seminar
series, our Trainees will be poised to make a meaningful impact on society through biomedicine.

application_id: 11071495
activity_code: F32
title: Long-range exchange of expectation, experience and error during auditory-motor learning
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Sensations we perceive arise from both the external environment as well as our own actions, and we learn to expect that certain movements will elicit predictable acoustic consequences. However, the same motor action can often lead to different sensory outcomes, and it is known that the suppression of self-generated sounds in the auditory cortex is highly sensory specific, but the degree to which the motor system reflects both the execution of an action, and its anticipated sensory outcome, is unclear. This proposal will provide valuable insights into the brain’s mechanisms for predicting and updating its encoding of self-generated sounds in real time and uncover fundamental principles underlying the dynamic information flow between sensory and motor regions of the brain.

application_id: 11194235
activity_code: F32
title: Investigating the role of tip link biophysics on MET function
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS EYE AND EAR INFIRMARY
phr: Project Narrative:
Hair cells are responsible for the transduction of sound and acceleration stimuli relevant to the auditory and
vestibular sensory systems, respectively. Critically, when the “hairs” or stereocilia of the hair cells are deflected,
tip link proteins connecting the stereocilia are pulled upon, opening the mechanoelectrial transduction (MET) ion
channels and allowing for excitatory cationic current to enter the hair cells. In this study, recent advances in gene
therapy techniques are leveraged to investigate the effects of modulating biophysical properties of the tip link
proteins on MET current, which will lead to a better understanding of the basic mechanisms mediating the
mechanotransduction function in hair cells.

application_id: 11164973
activity_code: F32
title: Auditory perceptual processing in the primate superior colliculus
org_name: STATE COLLEGE OF OPTOMETRY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This is a basic research proposal focused on the neural mechanisms of auditory perception. The overarching goal is to determine the role of the midbrain superior colliculus in a particularly important computational process - the setting of decision criteria - that lies at the heart of most models of auditory perception and communication but about which very little is known in terms of its instantiation in the primate brain. The results, therefore, will provide fundamental insights into the neural mechanisms of auditory perception and communication that, in the long term, may help to inform assays, treatments, or technologies focused on auditory perceptual and communication disorders.

application_id: 10986138
activity_code: K23
title: Impact of Hourly Neurochecks in Critically Ill Older Adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative Frequent brain examinations are an important tool for monitoring patients after brain injury, but if excessive, may cause sleep disruption and worsened outcomes in hospitalized patients. Interrupting sleep may make people confused (delirious) and may ultimately increase risk for dementia similar to Alzheimer’s disease. Improving patients’ sleep in the intensive care unit may improve their outcomes, so this project studies whether checking brain status hourly versus every-other-hour impacts sleep quantity and short or long-term cognitive outcomes.

application_id: 11061065
activity_code: F32
title: Tissue-engineered regeneration of the minipig TMJ condyle
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative
Temporomandibular joint (TMJ) condyle degeneration affects approximately 50M Americans, and tissue-
engineered implants have the potential to regenerate osteochondral defects on the TMJ condyle. This proposal
will characterize the TMJ condyle of the Yucatan minipig, engineer neocartilage-bone implants with robust
interfacial properties, and regenerate osteochondral defects of the TMJ condyle in a large minipig model.
Successful completion of this proposal will enhance translation of neocartilage-bone implants toward human use
by showing safety and efficacy in an in vivo model, modeled after FDA preclinical definitive animal studies.

title: Diagnose and Differentiate Dental Pain from Stress Using a Wearable Electrodermal Activity Device
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT STORRS
phr: Project Narrative
Our proposed work seeks to improve the efficiency and accuracy of endodontic diagnosis even for
those who have communication challenges, such as children, using quantifiable physiomarkers of pain.
Dental caries is one of the major causes of endodontic infection in the pulp or soft tissues of the tooth
and is estimated that at least 2 billion adults and 520 million children suffer from dental caries worldwide.
Endodontic infection can cause hypersensitivity, severe pain, and even death, but its diagnosis is
currently difficult and dependent on qualitative patient input.

title: Impact of Titanium-mediated Oxidative Stress on LPS/TLR4 Signaling
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Dysregulation of oral mucosal homeostasis is a causative factor underlying peri-implant inflammatory disease. title: Workshops in Formal Demography

This educational program is designed to train the next generation of population researchers in

title: Diagnose and Differentiate Dental Pain from Stress Using a Wearable Electrodermal Activity Device
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT STORRS
phr: Project Narrative Our proposed work seeks to improve the efficiency and accuracy of endodontic diagnosis even for those who have communication challenges, such as children, using quantifiable physiomarkers of pain. Dental caries is one of the major causes of endodontic infection in the pulp or soft tissues of the tooth and is estimated that at least 2 billion adults and 520 million children suffer from dental caries worldwide. Endodontic infection can cause hypersensitivity, severe pain, and even death, but its diagnosis is currently difficult and dependent on qualitative patient input.

title: 5-HT NEURONS AND MEAL REGULATION
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Despite the rapidly growing rate of obesity, the development of safe and effective therapeutics has
become progressively more promising, yet still limited, largely due to insufficient understanding of how
the brain manages body weight and feeding. Serotonin remains an excellent potential target for an anti-
obesity therapeutic, but there is a gap in understanding of the most effective way to utilize serotonin as
a tool for weight loss. This proposal will focus on how 5-HT (serotonin) neurons are regulated by
dopamine and/or GABA, and how these inputs contribute to the meal initiation.

title: Internalization of dead cells governs intestinal stem cell activity
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Intestinal health relies on an intact single layer of barrier cells that originate from a progenitor population of intestinal stem cells. How these stem cells sense environmental cues in order to make adjustments during infection and damage to maintain a healthy intestinal barrier is poorly understood. This proposal will establish a fundamental mechanism where intestinal stem cells take up dead neighboring cells to regulate their growth, turnover and differentiation, thereby establishing a new for therapeutic discovery in a variety of inflammatory intestinal diseases.

application_id: 11058406
activity_code: F32
title: Regulation of Enteric Nervous System Function by Group 3 Innate Lymphoid Cells
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The enteric nervous system is the largest collection of peripheral neurons that function independently of the
central nervous system and are critical for controlling key intestinal functions, including motility, nutrient handling,
and immune regulation, which have been shown to be dysregulated in the context of inflammatory bowel disease
(IBD), irritable bowel disorder (IBS), colon cancer, or during enteric infection. Therefore, a deeper understanding
of the mechanisms by which the intestinal immune cells impact the function of enteric neurons is essential for
elucidating the link between these two systems. This proposal will investigate the mechanism by which intestinal
innate lymphoid promote ENS homeostasis during states of health and disease.

application_id: 11066413
activity_code: F32
title: Diagnosing disparities in existing and proposed allocation systems for kidney transplantation
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
To address inequitable allocation, the kidney allocation system was changed in early 2021, and given the
complex interplay of disparities in allocation, this change may have unintended consequences for vulnerable
populations. Our goal is to develop robust equity metrics to study the impact of the new allocation system, both
in ensuring equitable distribution of organs to transplant centers across the US based on patient priority on the
waitlist, which was the aim of the policy change, and the new policy’s impact on kidney transplant rate for
vulnerable populations who have historically been disenfranchised by allocation policy. Our results will guide
the development of a more equitable allocation system.

application_id: 11142952
activity_code: F32
title: Intrauterine mechanisms for liver metabolic disease in the growth-restricted fetus
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Offspring born with fetal growth restriction (FGR) have increased incidence of metabolic dysfunction-associated
steatotic liver disease (MASLD) and periportal fibrosis. The goal of this proposal is to advance our understanding
of the mechanism for increased periportal collagen deposition, a sign of developing fibrosis, in the liver of the
fetus with FGR and the impact on future liver metabolic disease. Results from this proposal will be used to help
understand how fibrosis develops in postnatal liver of FGR offspring and determine future mechanistic
experiments to investigate fetal origins of hepatic fibrosis.

title: The role of diminished CNS hypoglycemia sensing in postbariatric hypoglycemia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Bariatric surgery is currently one of the most effective treatments for sustained weight loss and improved glycemia in individuals with obesity and type 2 diabetes. Despite the metabolic benefits of bariatric surgery, one third of patients suffer from low blood sugar (hypoglycemia), which when left untreated, can lead to cognitive deficits, serious accidents, and even sudden death. This project will investigate if hypoglycemia sensing within the brain is blunted after bariatric surgery, leading to improper physiological responses to raise blood glucose back to safe levels.
