title: New insights into the molecular regulation of mechanotransduction
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
Mechanotransduction is the molecular process by which cells sense and respond to mechanical signals in their
environment. The proposed work will use a new experimental platform to dissect the molecular interactions that
regulate mechanotransduction in adult physiology and disease. Using a novel experimental approach to
challenge the current dogma about the molecular basis of mechanotransduction, we will gain important insight
into how these pathways are disrupted in human disease, including cardiovascular disease.

application_id: 11171334
activity_code: F32
title: Incentive Payments and Practice Patterns in Dialysis: The Case of Calcimimetics
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
 Many patients with kidney failure on dialysis experience abnormal bone metabolism, placing them at higher
risk for death, cardiovascular events, and fractures, and may necessitate removal of the parathyroid glands.
This condition is treated with a class of medications called calcimimetics, which before 2018, over 20% of
dialysis patients could not access because of how they were paid for through Medicare. This project will
evaluate a) whether a Medicare policy change enhanced access to these expensive medications and b)
whether this translated to improvements in important patient outcomes, with broader implications for
understanding how health policies have the potential to influence disease burden in dialysis patients.

activity_code: F32
title: Development of coarse-to-fine spatial processing throughout the primary visual system
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OREGON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Psychophysical studies show humans perceive low spatial frequency stimuli more quickly than high spatial frequency stimuli, illustrating coarse-to-fine like processing. By studying the neural circuits and mechanisms underlying the development of coarse-to-fine spatial frequency processing in mice, this project will add to our understanding of how functional visual circuits develop through early postnatal life. The results of this project will shed new light on the circuits mediating spatial vision and may be instructive for a complete understanding of developmental diseases such as amblyopia.

title: Understanding the Role of GARP Proteins in Rod Outer Segment Disc Formation and Retinal Degeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In the retina, opsin molecules that capture light are housed within disc-shaped membranes stacked in the outer segment compartment of photoreceptor neurons. Many inherited retinal diseases are caused by defects in formation of the disc membranes within the outer segment, leading to photoreceptor cell death and human blindness. The purpose of this proposal is to investigate components that contribute to forming these disc membranes to better understand the pathogenesis of inherited retinal degeneration and identify potential treatment targets.

activity_code: K99
title: Cell type-specific mechanisms of history-dependent perceptual biases in sensory cortex
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The ability to flexibly prioritize stimuli based on their ongoing behavioral relevance is critical for thriving in a complex environment. Behavioral flexibility is commonly affected in many neurodevelopmental conditions including attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and autism spectrum disorders, but the cell type-specific neural circuits underlying these processes are not well understood. The proposed project will use modern genetic tools in mice to mechanistically test the role of local interneurons and long-range cholinergic inputs in gating flexible modulation of sensory cortex and behavior in response to the history of stimuli, actions and rewards experienced in the recent past.

title: Determining the role of the mechano-activated potassium channel TRAAK at nodes of Ranvier
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Narrative This proposal aims to determine whether the action potential operates as a mechanical, as well as electrical, pulse in myelinated axons. The planned research uses photoswitchable tethered ligands to control the mechanosensitive two-pore domain potassium channel TRAAK in order to investigate its contributions to action potential repolarization and propagation in myelinated axons of the optic nerve. This work has implications for the study of electric signaling, as a field, as well as for diseases of the node of Ranvier (e.g., multiple sclerosis, stroke, glaucoma).

title: Defining the mechanisms of injury resistance and role in restoring mucociliary transport of the airway hillock
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT SUMMARY / ABSTRACT
The airway epithelial surface is constantly exposed to the environment and serves as a barrier to many types
of injury - toxins, infections, allergens, and other insults. The airway epithelial surface must regenerate after
denuding injury. Both these aspects – barrier function and regeneration – can become dysregulated in
diseases such as asthma, COPD, and bronchiectasis. We have discovered a new airway epithelial cell type,
called the hillock, which survives various airway injuries and regenerates the epithelial surface to restore
mucociliary transport. The overall hypothesis of this proposal is that the hillock plays a critical role in barrier
function and regeneration in the airway and may become dysregulated in disease. Through single cell RNA
sequencing and ...

application_id: 10929521
activity_code: K23
title: Abdominal Pain in Older Patients in Emergency Departments
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Abdominal pain in patients over 65 years old is very common, causing 1.4 million emergency department visits per year. Older adults who develop abdominal pain severe enough to seek care at an emergency department have poor health outcomes, including death, as often as patients who develop a heart attack. This proposal seeks to understand how care of this patient population is managed—especially for patients with impaired cognition, Alzheimer's disease and related dementias, and/or frailty—so that it can ultimately be understood and improved.

title: Characterizing the Unique Biophysical Properties of the CBC2 OFF Cone Bipolar Cell
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Bipolar cells are the first interneurons of the retina primarily responsible for sampling photoreceptor input and distributing it into ON and OFF visual streams, yet new research suggests that each bipolar cell subtype collects and transforms different salient components of the visual scene (luminance, frequency, contrast, etc.) in different ways. This project will use super-resolution microscopy, Ca2+ imaging, electrophysiological techniques, and biophysical modeling to examine distinctive synaptic mechanisms in CBC2 Type 2 OFF cone bipolar cells and determine their unique contribution to overall retinal function. This knowledge is vital for producing a more comprehensive understanding of retinal physiology and developing new solutions to address human visual dysfunction...

phr: Project Narrative
This project will support an Academic Career Development Workshop for American Indian and Alaska Native
(AI/AN) students and early-career professionals at the 2025 Association of American Indian Physicians (AAIP)
Annual Meeting. The workshop will enhance access to mentorship, research education, and NIH funding
pathways, supporting AI/AN trainees as they pursue careers in biomedical research and medicine. This

with the ultimate goal of improving health in diabetes, obesity, and related metabolic conditions that
disproportionately affect this population.

title: Enabling mRNA Therapies for Pediatric High-Grade Gliomas
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: Diffuse midline glioma (DMG) is a lethal form of brain tumors that occurs in the brain stem region of children and young adults. DMG is an exceptionally challenging cancer to treat because of its unique cellular features and location, resulting in a median survival time of 9-12 months and a 5-year survival rate that is nearly 0%. This proposal aims to develop drug delivery technologies that can facilitate mRNA therapies for DMG.

application_id: 10807236
activity_code: K23
title: The Effect of Exercise on T Cell Aging in Rheumatoid Arthritis
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative Despite the clear beneficial effects of physical fitness and exercise to improving health during aging, we do not know the best amounts and intensities for optimal lifelong immune health. This project will address gaps in understanding how exercise improves immune system health in older patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA) by studying 1) how exercise training alters RA immune cell function, immune cell metabolism, and markers of premature immune cell aging; and 2) if a remotely supervised high-intensity exercise training program for older RA patients is safe, acceptable, and feasible. Results from this project will lead to novel treatments to improve inflammation and immune system health during aging.

application_id: 11018410
activity_code: R43
title: Diversity Supplement for Development of an Oscillated Insertion tool to Eliminate Surgically Induced Neurodegeneration for Optical Neuroimaging of Cognitive Aging and Dementia
org_name: ACTUATED MEDICAL, INC.
phr: Project Narrative: Relevance – Age related cognitive decline creates substantial emotional and financial burden as well as reduced quality of life for patients and their families. Better tools are needed for study of these conditions in preclinical models. Implanted devices generate a chronic inflammatory state caused by vascular perforation and crushing of brain tissue which leads to encapsulation of the implanted device by scar tissue and loss of neural density. This SBIR project develops the Optical Neuroscience Insertion Tool (ONIT) for reduced insertion force and tissue damage during placement of optical imaging systems (fiber optic cannula, GRIN lenses) in preclinical studies.

application_id: 10985001
activity_code: K23
title: Adapting and Testing the Care Partner Hospital Assessment Tool (CHAT) for Use in Dementia Care
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Lack of caregiving preparedness is prominent and persistent among care partners of patients living with Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) and associated with increased risk for adverse clinical outcomes for patients living with ADRD, and increased levels of burden, depression, and morbidity for care partners. Engaging key stakeholders to adapt the standardized Care Partner Hospital Assessment Tool for hospital-based dementia care will expand its potential reach. Further, findings from this pilot randomized controlled trial will enable future efficacy trials to advance the refinement and implementation of the decision- support tool that can enhance caregiving preparedness to improve health outcomes after hospital discharge.

application_id: 11240322
activity_code: K23
title: Adapting and Testing the Care Partner Hospital Assessment Tool (CHAT) for Use in Dementia Care
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Lack of caregiving preparedness is prominent and persistent among care partners of patients living with
Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) and associated with increased risk for adverse clinical
outcomes for patients living with ADRD, and increased levels of burden, depression, and morbidity for care
partners. Engaging key stakeholders to adapt the standardized Care Partner Hospital Assessment Tool for
hospital-based dementia care will expand its potential reach. Further, findings from this pilot randomized
controlled trial will enable future efficacy trials to advance the refinement and implementation of the decision-
support tool that can enhance caregiving preparedness to improve health outcomes after hospital discharge.

application_id: 10827198
activity_code: F32
title: Contributions of cortical feedback to orientation-dependent surround suppression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project Narrative Precise patterns of connectivity within and across brain regions drive the perception of visual stimuli — disrup- tion of these patterns underlie a number of complex psychiatric and neurological diseases. Important advances have elucidated neural architectures within single brain regions, but neural connectivity across brain regions is still deeply understudied, particularly in an intact animal. This proposal leverages a breakthrough two-photon holographic mesoscope — that can uniquely record and manipulate individual neurons across brain regions — to causally interrogate how interareal neural connections govern the cortical computations supporting perception.

application_id: 11095955
activity_code: K23
title: A stakeholder derived intervention to enhance technology engagement and reduce the emotional divide in distance caregiving of older adults with cognitive impairment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative
Cognitive impairment results in decreased ability to complete daily activities such as managing medications,
finances, and appointments resulting in care needs. However, one out of four individuals with cognitive
impairment lives alone and requires distance caregiving, which is associated with unique challenges compared
to in-person caregiving. This study involves developing an integrative stakeholder (care receivers with cognitive
impairment and their remote caregivers) co-designed intervention focused on improving use of innovative
remote caregiver technology, everyday functioning and independence of care receivers, remote caregiver
involvement, and the emotional bond between the dyad.

application_id: 11132591
activity_code: R25
title: Tobacco Treatment Training for Cancer Care Providers
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative
The purpose of the proposed renewal of this cancer education training project is to train oncology care
providers to implement tobacco use assessment and treatment (TUAT) in their cancer care settings. This goal
remains important because approximately 10-30% of cancer patients are smokers when diagnosed, and
approximately 70% of patients will continue to smoke after their diagnosis. Building on the successful
educational accomplishments of the past funding period, this project will implement and evaluate a TUAT skills
development course and collaborative training initiative that will train and provide technical assistance to
oncology care professionals to implement TUAT at their respective institutions.

phr: The Texas Research Experience eXperiment (TREX) project will establish partnerships for the development of high school curricular modules and career development activities designed to stimulate interest in and preparation for careers in science, technology, engineering, and medicine (STEM) through longitudinal mentoring of teachers and students by university faculty and summer research internships for students in faculty laboratories. application_id: 11086626
activity_code: K23
title: Use of Digital Phenotyping to Understand Digital Media Influence on Adolescent Substance Use
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Adolescent-onset substance use disorders are linked to poorer psychosocial outcomes and are predictive of mortality in early adulthood. For this reason, it is important to identify social and environmental factors that increase an adolescent’s risk of using substances and address those factors in clinical guidance and treatment planning. This study proposes the use of digital phenotyping technology to examine the complex and growing influence of digital media, including online exposure to substance-related content, on adolescent use and procurement of drugs and alcohol.

activity_code: K23
title: A stakeholder derived intervention to enhance technology engagement and reduce the emotional divide in distance caregiving of older adults with cognitive impairment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative Cognitive impairment results in decreased ability to complete daily activities such as managing medications, finances, and appointments resulting in care needs. However, one out of four individuals with cognitive impairment lives alone and requires distance caregiving, which is associated with unique challenges compared to in-person caregiving. This study involves developing an integrative stakeholder (care receivers with cognitive impairment and their remote caregivers) co-designed intervention focused on improving use of innovative remote caregiver technology, everyday functioning and independence of care receivers, remote caregiver involvement, and the emotional bond between the dyad.

application_id: 11118826
activity_code: K23
title: Serological markers of natural immunity to Plasmodium falciparum infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Plasmodium falciparum, the most common species of malaria, kills more children than any other pathogen, and the development of a highly effective vaccine has been challenging. Children acquire immunity to malaria with repeated exposure, but the mechanism is poorly understood. We will collect samples from children who are exposed to Plasmodium-infected mosquito bites and compare the immune responses among those who develop infection to those who do not to identify possible targets for vaccines to prevent malaria infection.

title: Accelerating digital cognitive screening for Alzheimer's disease in the Primary Care Setting
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: The validation and implementation of efficient, cost-effective screening tools that can accurately detect the earliest stages of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) in primary care settings is of paramount importance for the advancement of AD clinical care and prevention research. The objective of this study is to assess the feasibility, reliability and validity of novel digital cognitive screening tools for remote and in-clinic assessment. Our goal is to advance new technologies that are sensitive to detect subtle AD-associated cognitive decline, require minimal training to administer, have low patient burden, and are acceptable and accurate in older adult populations.

title: The Structural and Biochemical Effects of XFG-Associated Variants in LOXL1
org_name: GEORGIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Genetic variants in the protein LOXL1 can alter individual risk for developing pseudoexfoliation glaucoma, a blinding disease that affects more than 10 million individuals worldwide. This project seeks to determine how these genetic variants alter the structure and function of LOXL1. Successful completion of this project will provide a better understanding of how LOXL1 variants contribute to pseudoexfoliation glaucoma and thereby provide important groundwork for therapeutic development.

application_id: 11077188
activity_code: F32
title: The effects of sleep and iPRGCs on computations in the early visual system
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE/RELEVANCE
 Mammalian rod, cone, and melanopsin photoreceptors contribute to image-forming visual processes that
encode complex computations in the retina and cortex. Vision also influences behaviors like sleep, which result
in feedback mechanisms that enable sleep as a neuromodulator to the cortical neural code. Understanding how
multiplexed photoreceptor systems and sleep modulate cortical computations driven by complex visual stimuli
could lead to the development of more sophisticated computational models that help improve the design of
cortical prostheses.

org_name: JACKSON LABORATORY
phr: RELEVANCE TO PUBLIC HEALTH
The Jackson Laboratory proposes to initiate, with partners in higher education, a multi-state teacher education
program to provide genomics, bioinformatics and bioethics instruction to students preparing to enter the teaching

application_id: 11140311
activity_code: R25
title: Origins of Renal Physiology
org_name: MOUNT DESERT ISLAND BIOLOGICAL LAB
phr: Relevance
The course Origins of Renal Physiology is highly relevant to biomedical research and the advancement of human
health. The number of annual participants (up to 36; 25 of whom will be funded through this grant) represents
approximately 10% of the annual “class” of renal fellows in the United States, permitting the course to have a
significant impact on the training of fellows nationally. Course faculty include clinical nephrologists and
investigators who guide participants in performing experiments to elucidate kidney function including glomerular
filtration, proximal tubule, thick ascending limb, and collecting duct transport, water homeostasis, cellular
trafficking of transporter proteins, and the structure-function of transporter proteins such as aquaporins, urea
carriers, renal...

application_id: 11171329
activity_code: F32
title: Regulation of IGF-1R in acute and chronic thyroid eye disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative
Thyroid eye disease is a debilitating and disfiguring condition featuring deposition of hyaluronan and massive
tissue fibrosis leading to proptosis, eye muscle restriction, eyelid retraction, dry eye, and optic nerve compression.
The molecular basis of the disease is not fully understood, and neither is the regulation of the main signaling
axis upon which clinical trials and the new gold standard of treatment is based. Studying the cellular and
molecular basis of thyroid eye disease and its key cell surface proteins will identify novel treatment paradigms
and targets to improve the treatment of acute and chronic thyroid eye disease.

The validation and implementation of efficient, cost-effective screening tools that can accurately detect the earliest stages of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) in primary care settings is of paramount importance for the advancement of AD clinical care and prevention research. activity_code: F31
title: Positive Childhood Experiences and Intergenerational Trauma Transmission
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) exert a significant, detrimental impact on adult health outcomes, while positive childhood experiences (PCEs) have been shown to have a protective effect, even in the presence of ACEs. Less is known, however, about the specific mechanisms of intergenerational transmission of parental ACEs and PCEs to their children’s ACEs and the impact on parenting practices. The results of the proposed study will provide additional insight on parental transmission of both positive and adverse childhood experiences, parenting practices, and opportunities to translate findings into community interventions.

title: Defining the mechanism of PTCHD1 and membrane cholesterol in the regulation of the mu-opioid receptor
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative GPCRs are a large family of signaling receptors that mediate many biological functions, including analgesia in the context of the mu-opioid receptor. We recently identified the cholesterol sequestering protein, PTCHD1, as a negative regulator of the mu-opioid receptor. title: PATHS
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: Providing the education community and students with knowledge of the various health
professions and multiple ways to reach these professions is a critical need of public health. In addition, an understanding of the various social impacts on health (Social Determinants of Health) and use of data science tools modeled after Precision Health’s use of large data will inform teachers and students of how these tools may inform healthcare. PATHS will develop curriculum materials and deliver professional development designed to support teachers and their students and will provide an array of supports for community-engaged projects, such as health fairs.

application_id: 11159829
activity_code: F32
title: Stereoselective Transition Metal Catalysis Enabled by Hydrogen-Bond Donor Mediated Electrophile Activation
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
 The development of catalytic methods for the synthesis of complex molecules from simple building blocks
ultimately impacts the efficiency and cost at which pharmaceutical drugs can be made. This proposal outlines
the development and mechanistic interrogation of a new method for the synthesis of enantioenriched amine-
containing compounds, which are ubiquitous motifs found in many life-saving medicines. It is anticipated that the
successful execution of the proposed research will reveal a general method for the conversion of simple building
blocks to valuable products, which will be of interest to practicing chemists engaged in the synthesis of bioactive
molecules.

activity_code: K23
title: Early life protein-enriched human milk diets to increase lean body mass accretion and diversity of the gut microbiome in extremely preterm infants: a randomized trial
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Premature infants born at the limits of viability usually do not grow well during their hospital stay and have a higher risk of developing long-term nutritional complications related to extreme prematurity. Early administration of protein-enriched human milk diets could improve growth by promoting lean body mass accretion, stimulating maturation of gastrointestinal tract, and modifying the gut microbiome. We will test whether protein-enriched human milk diets during the first 2 weeks after birth in premature infants born at the limits of viability result in more lean body mass accretion and more diversity of the gut microbiome by the time of hospital discharge.

title: Cortical mechanisms underlying aberrant auditory perception
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Tinnitus (e.g., ringing in the ears) and hyperacusis (hypersensitivity to sound) are highly prevalent auditory

title: Control of gene expression via RNA-targeted covalent probes
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative RNA-targeting chemistries are the foundation for new classes of therapeutics that circumvent challenges associated with protein-centered drug discovery. application_id: 10996389
activity_code: F32
title: Cellular Mechanisms of Acute-Intermittent Hypercapnic-Hypoxia Induced Phrenic Motor Plasticity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE My focus concerns plasticity in the neural system controlling breathing, specifically phrenic long-term facilitation (pLTF) following acute intermittent hypoxia (AIH). I propose to uncover mechanism(s) of heightened pLTF when comparing acute intermittent hypercapnic-hypoxia (AIHH) versus AIH without hypercapnia by testing the hypothesis that AIHH enhances plasticity by optimizing the balance between competing pathways to phrenic motor facilitation. Pursuing this research will train me in new methods and ways of thinking, advance our understanding of respiratory motor plasticity, and may impact clinical trial design since AIH is being explored as a treatment to improve respiratory and non-respiratory motor function in people with spinal cord injuries and ALS.

application_id: 10914690
activity_code: F30
title: Quantifying the interactions among maternal race, vaginal metabolites, and microbes in preterm birth
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Narrative Preterm birth is the leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality, is frequently associated with maternal morbidity, and is significantly more common among Black women. The interactions of race with mechanisms that cause preterm birth and on potential preterm birth biomarkers are not fully understood. I will conduct a paired analysis of vaginal metabolites and metagenomics in a racially diverse cohort of pregnant women to quantify the interactions among race, vaginal metabolites, vaginal microbes, and preterm birth, in order to address a significant reproductive health challenge and driver of racial inequity.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Dysfunctions in airway motile cilia, hair-like organelles that line the respiratory tract, are implicated in pulmonary disorders such as SARS-CoV2 infection, as well as ciliopathies that have devastating effects on multiple organ systems; however, although cilia play an integral role in airway innate immunity, the biological processes underlying the formation and maintenance of these organelles are still poorly understood. application_id: 11294264
activity_code: F32
title: Defining the biological roles of the 2',3'-cyclic phosphatases Angel1 and Angel2
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative
 Due to the many varied and critical roles that RNA plays in the cell, ensuring the fidelity of the
cell’s transcriptome is of utmost importance and an emerging field of RNA regulation has been the
presence of RNA modifications. This research fellowship will investigate the biological role of an
understudied 3’-end RNA modification known as 2’,3’-cyclic phosphate and how this modification is
regulated by the cyclic phosphatases Angel1 and Angel2. Overall, my research will help to unravel
previously understudied role that the 2’,3’>P modification plays in regulating the fidelity of the
transcriptome.

There is a high need for qualified multidisciplinary scientists trained in rigorous research methods related to “real-world” addiction treatment needs in communities most impacted by substance use disorders (SUDs) and substance-related morbidity and mortality. application_id: 10904686
activity_code: F32
title: Defining the biological roles of the 2',3'-cyclic phosphatases Angel1 and Angel2
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative  Due to the many varied and critical roles that RNA plays in the cell, ensuring the fidelity of the cell’s transcriptome is of utmost importance and an emerging field of RNA regulation has been the presence of RNA modifications. This research fellowship will investigate the biological role of an understudied 3’-end RNA modification known as 2’,3’-cyclic phosphate and how this modification is regulated by the cyclic phosphatases Angel1 and Angel2. Overall, my research will help to unravel previously understudied role that the 2’,3’>P modification plays in regulating the fidelity of the transcriptome.

activity_code: F32
title: The role of cis-regulatory elements in the inheritance of transcriptional memory through mitosis.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Mitosis is a pivotal point during which a cell divides and gives rise to two genetically identical daughter cells. However, transcriptional programs must also be transmitted to ensure cell identity is properly re-established with each cell division. This proposal will study the molecular mechanisms that allow cells to maintain and faithfully re-establish the proper magnitude of gene expression through mitosis towards understanding how cell fate and identity is encoded.

application_id: 10899557
activity_code: K23
title: The influence of air pollution on lung health among people living with HIV.
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Most global COPD morality occurs in regions of the world where smoking is uncommon such as sub-Saharan Africa – home to an estimated 26 million people with COPD – where the converging HIV and air pollution epidemics may be driving COPD mortality. This project characterizes personal air pollution exposure among 300 people with HIV and 300 HIV-uninfected comparators in Uganda and uses spirometry and research bronchoscopy to examine whether HIV serostatus influences relationships between air pollution, inflammation and lung function. Findings from this study will inform future interventions designed to improve lung health among these at-risk populations by targeting the candidate mechanistic pathways identified in this proposal.

application_id: 11089291
activity_code: F32
title: Symmetry Breaking in Cell Assemblies
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
Epithelial cells are essential for organismal development and homeostasis and require precise
coordination of polarity information to properly form and function. A wealth of studies have
uncovered proteins required for cell polarity, however, little is known about how these core
protein complexes 'break symmetry' to initiate and define asymmetric domains within cell
collectives. The proposed work will shed light on fundamental cellular processes essential for
the formation and maintenance of epithelial tissues and will help better understand the normal
and disease states of epithelial cells.

title: Pharmaceutical Sciences and Pharmacogenomics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: The current study will shed mechanistic light into understanding the role of fibroblast growth factor 15 and 19 (FGF15/19), mainly induced by farnesoid X receptor in the small intestine by bile acids, in regulating drug metabolizing enzyme gene expression and activating a xenobiotic nuclear receptor, constitutive androstane receptor, to regulate liver drug metabolism. title: Defining the role of TgATG9 in Toxoplasma gondii autophagy and persistence
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Up to one third of the global human population is chronically infected with the protozoan parasite Toxoplasma gondii which can lead to fatal reactivated toxoplasmosis in immune-compromised patients or recurrent ocular disease in otherwise healthy people. The cellular processes mediating Toxoplasma persistence are largely unknown, which precludes strategic development of effective therapies to eliminate chronic-stage parasites. application_id: 11091990
activity_code: F32
title: Photoredox-enabled deracemization: method development and mechanistic studies
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
 The proposed work describes a light-driven deracemization method for compounds containing benzylic motifs
commonly found in pharmaceuticals and mechanistic investigations of that system. Our approach employs a photocatalytic
stereoablation process that generates a prochiral intermediate that undergoes asymmetric proton transfer by a chiral co-
catalyst. Preliminary mechanistic data indicates that these types of deracemization reactions can occur with high
enantioenrichment even when the elementary steps that comprise these processes have undesirable degrees of
stereoselectivity.

application_id: 10899613
activity_code: F31
title: Establishing a Spatial Map of Dopamine Reward Prediction Error Computations and their Function in Distinct Associative Learning Processes Across the Striatum: a Methodological Framework
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Striatum dopamine dysfunction is implicated in a variety of neurological and psychiatric disorders including Parkinson’s Disease, Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder, schizophrenia, and addiction. However, how dopamine release dynamics in different regions of the striatum support such diverse functions is not well understood, hindering the development of more targeted and effective treatments. This project aims to understand how spatiotemporal variations in striatum-wide dopamine reward prediction error computations contribute to different types of learning with the goal of developing a more nuanced and complete understanding of dopamine function and dysfunction.

title: Sulfide-regulated copper metabolism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Hydrogen sulfide regulates energy metabolism by functioning as a respiratory inhibitor and a substrate. application_id: 11309082
activity_code: F32
title: A Data-Driven Approach to Protein Engineering of Aminotransferase ARO8
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative
Directed evolution campaigns of enzymes are critical to the production of modern pharmaceuticals but are
burdened by large search spaces and slow initial optimization. The optimization of biocatalysts for non-native
reactivity is greatly accelerated by rapid identification of optimal mutations. Using a data-driven approach, we
will develop a workflow to identify optimal sets of mutations of aminotransferase ARO8 that promote new C–C
bond forming reactions.

application_id: 11105768
activity_code: F32
title: Investigating mechanisms of coordinating actinstructure across multiple levels oforder
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Actin is a key component of the cellular cytoskeleton that enables cells and tissues to control their shape, and
thus is essential for force generation (for example, during embryonic development) and migration (for example,
during cancer extravasation), yet we lack the knowledge of how actin structures are coordinated across
multiple levels of organization. This project aims to determine how the protein Bitesize, in the Drosophila fly
model system, directly interacts with the actin protein to enable the individual actin molecule to assemble into
filaments, and to organize these filaments into larger, more intricate structures. Thus, this research seeks to

activity_code: F32
title: De novo design of photoacid-binding proteins to study proton dynamics in biological systems
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project narrative Biological energy transduction is crucial to life and is primarily orchestrated through the coupling of proton and electron movement. While electron transfer has been extensively studied, proton transfer has been more difficult to study experimentally. Through protein design, we will design and express proteins that bind abiological photoacid cofactors that will enable us to observe, for the first time, the generation and management of proton currents in proteins, giving fundamental insight into proton conduction in biological systems and take the first step toward the design of complex protein machinery.

activity_code: T32
title: Integrated Training in Engineering and Diabetes
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Diabetes is a complex disease, and understanding its complexity is critical for creating new clinical therapies to
control the current diabetes epidemic, better treat its complications, and lead to an eventual cure. Because
engineering approaches have proven useful for solving complex biomedical problems, we propose to train a
new multi-disciplinary work force with the skills needed to integrate both engineering and diabetes research
approaches. This will be accomplished through formal course work and an unconventional dual mentor
arrangement, where each student or postdoctoral trainee is supervised by one advisor with an engineering
background and another from the diabetes research field.

phr: 8. Project Narrative
Diseases caused by protozoan and helminth parasites and arthropod-vectored infectious diseases continue
to affect millions of people in resource-poor developing countries. The goal of the Parasitology and Vector
Biology training program is to recruit and train the next generation of investigators whose focus is on
understanding the basic mechanisms regulating host-parasite interactions as well as animal and human
parasite transmission. These future parasitology and vector biology research leaders will contribute to
global efforts to control these devastating diseases.

activity_code: F32
title: Gadusol: An ancient sunscreen that protects metazoan development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative Sunscreens that prevent UVR induced DNA damage are imperative for human health and protection against melanoma. This project will investigate the function of gadusol, a newly discovered vertebrate sunscreen. Our study will provide insight into the developmental mechanisms and evolutionary origin of this sunscreen, which could lead to the production of effective and environmentally safe sunscreens.

application_id: 10825817
activity_code: F30
title: IL-19 Modifies Lymphatic Flux During Hyperlipidemia
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: Current therapeutics for atherosclerosis fail to consider the role of inflammation and lymphatic system function, both of which have the potential to promote plaque stability. Through preliminary in vitro and in vivo models, we found that IL-19, an endogenous anti-inflammatory cytokine, induces the formation of functional lymphatic vessels by modifying their permeability, which has the potential to increase both white blood cell egression and reverse cholesterol transport. This grant will explore the mechanism of action behind IL-19’s effect on lymphatic system function in the setting of hypercholesterolemia.

application_id: 10996246
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the role of endothelial Gimap5 in maintaining liver homeostasis
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Liver disease is one of the highest causes of morbidity and mortality in the country, and by studying rare forms of liver disease evidence suggests we can better understand more common forms of disease. GIMAP5 is a protein that when mutated to cause a loss-of-function leads to dysfunction of the cells that compose blood vessels in the liver. By better understanding the mechanisms by which GIMAP5 causes blood vessel dysfunction, we can potentially discover novel treatments for patients with this rare disease as well as for more common forms of liver disease.

application_id: 11193214
activity_code: F32
title: Structure and Function of an ADAM10-Tspan5 Complex
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
A Disintegrin and Metalloprotease 10 (ADAM10) is an enzyme that catalyzes the ectodomain shedding of
critical signaling and adhesion proteins including Notch, amyloid precursor protein, and cadherins, and its
dysregulation is implicated in the development of varied pathologies including neurodegenerative diseases and
several cancers. ADAM10 is tightly regulated by the C8 subclass of tetraspanin proteins (TspanC8s) according
to mechanisms that are incompletely understood. This proposal aims to combine structural and functional
studies of an ADAM10-Tspan5 complex to elucidate mechanisms by which TspanC8s influence ADAM10
substrate selectivity.

application_id: 11182462
activity_code: F32
title: Examining the conditionality and time dynamics of evolutionary constraint in yeast
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Evolutionary constraints determine how organisms can adapt to multiple selection pressures and
therefore influence many evolutionary processes, including those with human health relevance like drug
resistance evolution, pathogen host range evolution, and cancer evolution. However, we still do not
well understand the determinants of such constraints and how constraints arising from different
processes change the dynamics of evolution. The proposed research will expand upon a high-
throughput yeast barcoding experimental evolution system to dissect the constraints underpinning
metabolic specialization and understand how they change over longer time scales, thereby answering
fundamental questions about the nature of evolutionary constraint and generating quantitative insight...

application_id: 10820424
activity_code: R25
title: Strengthening the Master of Health Research Ethics Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MALAYA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE To meet the need for increasing research ethics capacity in Malaysia and Southeast Asia, in partnership with collaborators from the Berman Institute of Bioethics at Johns Hopkins University, this initiative aims to enhance the leading-edge Master of Health Research Ethics (MOHRE) program at Universiti Malaya (UM). Program enhancements will include increasing program flexibility to increase enrolment of working individuals positioned to make meaningful contributions to research ethics, strengthening the program by using a newly articulated competency framework, and implementing a robust UM faculty development program. By the end of the project period, the MOHRE program will be more sustainable and be positioned to help meet local and regional needs in research ethics.

application_id: 11249936
activity_code: F32
title: Mechanisms of SAGA Regulation by Histone Crosstalk
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
The post-translational modification (PTM) of histones that package eukaryotic genomes into chromatin are
integral to the regulation of gene expression. The SAGA (Spt-ADA-GCN5-acetyltransferase) complex, capable
of H3 acetylation and H2B deubiquitination, is a transcriptional co-activator involved in nearly all Pol II
mediated transcription and contains two enzymatic complexes: the histone acetyltransferase (HAT) module
and deubiquitinating (DUB) module. In my studies, I am to bridge the gap in our mechanistic understanding of
SAGA’s regulation of transcription, such as how SAGA exerts its reader, writer, and eraser functions to regulate
gene expression on a poly-nucleosome template and how the SAGA HAT module acetylates chromatin to
promote active transcription.

application_id: 10996951
activity_code: F32
title: Long Term Outcomes and Cost-Implications of Inequitable Access to AcuteInpatient Rehabilitation
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative Patients with traumatic spinal cord injuries (SCI) risk irreversible functional decline if they do not undergo a timely program of supervised rehabilitation at an inpatient rehabilitation facility (IRF). However, recent work published by our group demonstrates that publicly-insured SCI patients are often not triaged to IRFs in comparison to privately-insured peers, even after controlling for risk factors such as age, comorbidities, and injury severity. The study here aims to quantify the economic costs and clinical complications associated with lack of IRF access and uses novel statistical and machine learning methods to predict the cost effectiveness of policy interventions for improving the care of this vulnerable population.

application_id: 10847429
activity_code: F30
title: Understanding the role of TWIST1 in colorectal cancer progression and metastasis
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative The vast majority of patients with metastatic colorectal cancer (CRC) cannot be cured, mainly due to a lack of understanding and therapeutics to target the underlying metastatic process. A significant barrier for metastasis research is recapitulating the complex biology in a suitable model system. This proposal aims to utilize state-of- the-art CRC organoids and orthotopic mouse models to understand the CRC metastatic process better, informing future research into therapeutics to improve the survival and quality of life for individuals with CRC.

application_id: 11189676
activity_code: R25
title: Frugal Science Academy: Training K-12 innovators and democratizing synthetic biology tools
org_name: GEORGIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
The goal in this work is to develop a new initiative, Frugal Science Academy (FSA), to empower
underrepresented minority students and teachers from rural schools in Georgia to engage in
synthetic biology, where the traditional bottleneck has been the lack of affordable scientific
equipment for hands-on STEM. The Frugal Science Academy will train young URM high school
investigators to develop “frugal hardware” (from concept to publication) used in synthetic biology
research and disseminate their research into underequipped labs while also training rural high
school teachers to incorporate synthetic biology education and frugal hardware into their curricula.
This work will increase the diversity, creativity, and innovation in the rapidly expanding field of
synthetic biology...

application_id: 10788427
activity_code: K01
title: Chromatin remodeling in GABA neurons contributes to alcohol use disorder
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Alcohol abuse affects millions of people in the United States, and individuals who are resistant to alcohol intoxication and sedation are more likely to develop alcohol use disorder. Chromatin remodeling in GABA neurons is one mechanism that may be involved in alcohol use disorders, because these mechanisms regulate downstream gene expression and neuronal function. I propose to investigate how chromatin in GABA neurons is altered by alcohol abuse, which will elucidate the role of GABA neurons in alcohol use disorder and will identify new therapeutic targets.

application_id: 10877777
activity_code: R90
title: University of Iowa Institutional Training Program in Oral Health Research
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative  The proposed continuation of our institutional oral health research training program is designed to provide  training opportunities at the PhD and post-doctoral levels for both dentist and non-dentist scientists, including  training in translational and clinical research, research rigor and reproducibility, and the responsible conduct of  research. This program is designed to allow trainees to develop the complex skills necessary to undertake  independent and collaborative research, and thus to become oral health research scholars capable of meeting  future opportunities and challenges in dental education and oral health research.

application_id: 10996310
activity_code: F31
title: Determining the role of sperm microRNAs in early development and non-genetic inheritance
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE It is becoming increasingly accepted that gametes transfer non-genetic information from parent to child that modulate inherited phenotypes. A species of non-coding RNA, microRNAs, delivered from sperm to egg during fertilization have been demonstrated to causally induce changes in early development ultimately leading to non-genetically altered adult phenotypes; however, the molecular mechanisms underlying this phenomenon are unknown. In this project, I will determine the initial functions of sperm microRNAs in regulating early development and uncover the molecular mechanism underlying the paternal transfer of non-genetic information in mammals.

activity_code: T32
title: Neurobiology of CNS Injury and Repair
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: Project Narrative Acute central nervous system (CNS) injuries such as spinal cord injury, traumatic brain injury, and stroke share many common cellular and molecular mechanisms of damage and repair. These mechanisms often differ substantially from other genetic, developmental, or degenerative brain diseases, necessitating specialized education and research training for those who pursue careers in CNS injury research with the goal of lessening the health-related burden of CNS injury on survivors. The Neurobiology of CNS Injury and Repair T32 Program is designed to provide outstanding, comprehensive educational and research-intensive training to predoctoral students and fully prepare them for their transition to independent careers.

title: Mechanistic insights into factor VIII inhibitor formation and eradication
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Hemophilia A is an inherited bleeding disorder caused by the lack of coagulation factor VIII (FVIII) and resulting in debilitating joint disease due to chronic bleeding. The immune response to replacement FVIII therapy is a significant concern that could be alleviated by better characterization of the factors that predispose to or regulate anti-FVIII antibody development. This proposal seeks to understand the pathogenesis of FVIII antibodies and investigates the mechanism of a potential novel regulator, B cell activating factor (BAFF), in FVIII immunogenicity with the goal of developing targeted therapies.

activity_code: R25
title: Quantitative Team Science (QuanTS) Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Quantitative Team Science (QuanTS) Program will provide training and mentorship in collaborative skills
to equip quantitative scientists to work effectively on research teams across all fields of medicine. To
accomplish this goal, we will create online didactic training materials that are broadly accessible to the
quantitative science community, facilitate an experiential, small-group mentorship program focused on team
science skills for early-career quantitative scientists, and provide guidance and training materials for mentors.
We will also integrate novel evaluation processes within the program to examine facilitators, barriers and
outcomes of the proposed methods for training and mentoring quantitative team scientists.

org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: The proposed R25 Science Education Partnership Award (SEPA) is a partnership between a research institution and an informal learning space to conduct STEM focused playgroups with a goal of empowering caregivers of young children to implement high-quality learning activities within their daily routines and activities. A longstanding challenge for modern medical practice is rapidly applying cutting-edge scientific discoveries into better diagnosis and treatments for patients. Training contemporary clinician-scientists in a highly integrated MD-PhD dual-degree program that places great emphasis on retaining program graduates into long-term research careers would, therefore, be of substantial benefit to the health and well-being of the nation's populations.

title: Investigating the role of signaling feedback in directed cell migration
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative Cell migration is ubiquitously important for development, homeostasis, and is altered in many diseases. application_id: 10912526
activity_code: F31
title: Single-cell Transcriptomic Analysis of Cell Type Plasticity in Barrel Cortex of Normal and Autism Model Mice
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Sensory experience during postnatal development refines an embryonic blueprint of the primary somatosensory cortex (S1), endowing it with an array of functions made possible by neural circuits composed of hundreds of cell types. In Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASDs), this experience-dependent plasticity is compromised, resulting in the impaired development of sensorimotor function. This project will combine single-nucleus mRNA sequencing, machine learning, and histology with sensory deprivations and a transgenic mouse model of autism to uncover the influence of ASD on experience-dependent cell type maturation and provide a framework for developing cell type-specific therapeutic interventions.

application_id: 11103279
activity_code: T32
title: Training Program in Cellular and Molecular Biology
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Most human diseases arise due to disruptions in basic cellular and molecular processes caused
by mutations in one's DNA or the presence of a pathogen in one's body. Our program trains
students in the core concepts and methods of cell and molecular biology, preparing them to
uncover basic insight into the cell and molecular processes that normally control cell growth and
development but that when perturbed can lead to diseased states. With better knowledge of
these processes and more trained biomedical scientists in the workforce, we can improve our
ability to detect, treat, and defeat many crippling human diseases.

application_id: 11178307
activity_code: F32
title: The role of cortactin-mediated actin branch stabilization in force-producing actin networks
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OREGON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Proteins that stabilize and destabilize branched actin networks are thought to be critical for force production by
actin networks, but these functions are difficult to isolate. Understanding how actin branches are regulated is
critical for understanding cell processes like endocytosis and cell motility. This proposal seeks to understand
the role of cortactin-mediated branch stabilization and targeted debranchers in (1) force production and (2)
recycling of monomeric actin, as well as (3) elucidating the role and activity of an enigmatic debranching
protein, coronin 7.

title: Enhanced cartilage formation of chondrocytes in viscoelastic ECMs under mechanical loading
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  New approaches are needed to mitigate and treat osteoarthritis, a debilitating disease involving a progressive erosion of the cartilage tissue, and one that lacks an effective treatment option barring a total surgical replacement of the joint. Emerging research shows that biomechanical loading of the joint can be an effective mitigation strategy of osteoarthritis and can accelerate the production of biomedically engineered cartilage tissue, yet how such loading processes result in improvements in the joint health remains unclear. phr: Project Narrative

activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of oxytocin signaling in the social regulation of eating
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative  Over 40% of adults in the USA are classified as overweight or obese and there is currently a shortage of effective treatments to reduce the excessive food intake that can lead to obesity. Novel and effective obesity prevention and treatment strategies are aided by basic science research identifying specific brain chemical systems that reduce food intake. A novel approach is taken here; we are investigating the extent to which factors including sex and social context modulate the effects of oxytocin, a neuropeptide with clinical relevance to human obesity treatment, on food intake regulation.

activity_code: F30
title: Unraveling the Cellular Dynamics of the Cranial Base Synchondroses Throughout Postnatal Craniofacial Development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Midfacial hypoplasia is a devastating condition caused by structural defects of the cranial base, resulting in difficulties breathing, chewing and often necessitating surgical correction. While it is understood that the cranial base promotes craniofacial growth, and that cartilage cells (chondrocytes) are required for its development, the molecular mechanisms that regulate these processes are unknown. The objective of this proposal is to define mechanisms by which cranial base chondrocytes function to promote growth of the midface in order to identify future therapeutic targets for the treatment of cranial base malformations.

application_id: 10998751
activity_code: F32
title: Understanding How Distinct Heterochromatin States Control Pioneer Factor Binding
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative Restriction of transcription factor (TF) binding by heterochromatin is essential for spatiotemporal control of gene expression and proper embryonic development. Despite developmental transitions from gene repression to activation being often initiated by pioneer factors which overcome the heterochromatin barrier, it remains poorly understood whether distinct types of heterochromatin differentially regulate DNA accessibility and TF binding. Here, I will directly compare how G9A/GLP and SETDB1, responsible for histone H3 Lysine 9 di and trimethylation, respectively, restrict TF and pioneer factor binding to the same gene regulatory sequences.

application_id: 11184173
activity_code: F32
title: Metabolic control of mitonuclear balance for OXPHOS protein biogenesis
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) complexes, which are critical for ATP synthesis and for
proper mitochondrial function, comprise subunits that are encoded by both the nuclear and the mitochondrial
genomes. These two genomes must be co-regulated to ensure stoichiometric production of OXPHOS subunits,
and to avoid mitonuclear imbalance, which can lead to mitochondrial disorders. This project will explore how
diverse metabolism-related factors regulate the balanced expression of the two genomes for stoichiometric
OXPHOS protein biogenesis, and uncover the link between metabolism and mitonuclear balance, identifying
potentially novel therapeutic targets for mitochondrial disorders.

application_id: 11140350
activity_code: F32
title: Ubiquitin-mediated regulation of kinetochore assembly and function
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Accurate chromosome segregation is essential to an organism’s growth and development. In eukaryotic cells,
chromosome segregation is facilitated by the kinetochore, a conserved multiprotein complex that mediates the
attachment of microtubules to chromosomes. This proposal seeks to identify and define the mechanisms of
ubiquitin-mediated regulation of the kinetochore; these findings will illuminate principles governing a central
biological process with implications for diseases marked by chromosome segregation errors.

title: A process evaluation of a pragmatic randomized controlled trial to promote mental health among adults with Type 1 diabetes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF GEORGIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE People with type 1 diabetes (T1D) have increased rates of depression which decreases the probability of meeting glycemic goals and increases the risk of diabetes care complications. application_id: 10899835
activity_code: F31
title: Role and regulation of a differentiation-specific keratin in keratinocyte adaptation to mechanical stress
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative The palmoplantar or glabrous epidermis is uniquely adapted to the greater mechanical stress it experiences in daily life, as compared to other body sites, and is characterized by a specific pattern of keratin intermediate filament (IF) expression including the glabrous-specific keratin 9 and the stress-inducible keratin 16. Keratin 9 levels are remarkably low in embryonic mice, despite abundant expression in adult mouse and human glabrous skin, and mice lacking keratin 9 develop thick glabrous lesions. This proposal will identify the temporal and spatial dynamics and role of keratin 9 expression during early postnatal life and investigate the contribution of mechanical stress, specifically tension and compression, to homeostasis in this unique epidermal location.

activity_code: F31
title: Divergent response to radio-immunotherapy in HNSCC and PDAC is mediated by regulatory T cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Narrative Head and neck cancer (HNSCC) and pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) exhibit dichotomous responses to combination radiation immunotherapy in the context of OX40 agonism, highlighted by decreased CD8 T cell effector function and enhanced regulatory T cell (Treg) suppression, resulting in diminished survival in PDAC models. The PDAC tumor microenvironment (TME) is rich in IL-33 and Tregs which express the cognate receptor ST2. We aim to determine the differential responses to OX40 agonism between HNSCC and PDAC, and whether the IL-33/ST2 axis on Tregs promotes a suppressive TME.

title: Highly parallelized characterization of histone reader dysfunction
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Proper interactions between chromatin and its regulatory factors are fundamental for essential biological processes such as DNA replication, repair, and transcription. Protein domains known as “readers” facilitate recruitment of regulators to specific regions of chromatin marked by histone posttranslational modifications, however the characterization of “histone reader” domains is far from complete and hinders our comprehension of their role in diseases. The goal of this project is to develop a technology platform that will enable rapid and systematic characterization of protein variant interaction with post-translational modifications and apply it to studying the fundamental properties and impact of disease mutations on histone reader function.

application_id: 11247037
activity_code: F32
title: Biocatalytic Synthesis of N-N and C-N Heterobiaryl Atropisomers
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Atropisomerism, or chirality that arises from restricted rotation about a bond, is an important design principle
in drug discovery due to its ability to increase the potency and binding selectivity of small-molecule drugs. N–N
and C–N heterobiaryl atropisomers demonstrate potent pharmaceutical properties, yet few methods exist for
their atroposelective synthesis. This proposal seeks to develop a biocatalytic strategy for the atroposelective
synthesis of these medicinally relevant classes of atropisomers.

title: Neurobiology of Paternal Care
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Caring fathers can directly support child physical health and behavioral development while abusive and neglectful fathers can prove harmful. It remains largely unknown at the neural level why some adult males demonstrate care while others demonstrate abuse or neglect, and there is little information on how these variable paternal phenotypes may be shaped by social and environmental experience in earlier development. The proposed research will use a biparental rodent model species to elucidate the neural activity and molecular mechanisms associated with natural variation in paternal care.

title: Deciphering the Role of Glycan Sulfation in Neuroinflammation and Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a common neurodegenerative disorder that affects every 1 in 9 Americans aged 65 and older. Mounting evidence links dysfunctional brain perineuronal nets (PNNs) and their sulfated glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) as an underlying driver of AD progression through the promotion of neurocircuit destabilization. The goal of this proposal is to determine novel mechanisms linking changes in brain PNN glycans sulfation patterning with neuroinflammation and loss of stable PNN matrices inherent to AD, thus providing a novel foundation upon which new targets and therapies for AD can be developed and tested.

title: Clinical Research in Digestive Diseases (CRDD) Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The University of Michigan's Clinical Research in Digestive Diseases (CRDD) Training Program
provides patient-oriented clinical research training to physician-scientists whose goal is to
improve the treatment of digestive diseases. The purpose of this training is to produce
outstanding clinically oriented medical researchers who investigate the causes, prevention, and
treatment of digestive diseases. Since 2003, our trainees have fulfilled this goal, and 95% of our
graduates are now faculty at academic medical centers, being awarded multiple research
grants, and producing numerous publications of their research work over the past 19 years of
funding, leading to improved care and outcomes in a broad range of digestive diseases.

title: A child's best friend: Behavioral, neural, and endocrinological mechanisms of longitudinal social bond formation and stress buffering effects in children and pet dogs
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Attachment bonds between children and family dogs can result in positive therapeutic outcomes in both typical and clinical populations, including reduction of child stress and anxiety when the dog is present. These outcomes are likely mediated by the strength of the child-dog bond, so identifying the biological mechanisms that support the formation of strong, successful bonds is foundational to maximizing these therapeutic effects. The current proposal longitudinally examines the development of behavioral, neural, and endocrine mechanisms that underlie the stress- and anxiety-reducing effects of social bonds between children and their pet dogs.

title: The Role of Post-Translational Modifications in Bacterial Responses to Methylglyoxal
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative This project will use Escherichia coli to explore new ways that bacteria protect themselves from methylglyoxal (MGO), a toxic compound ubiquitously produced during central metabolism. In bacteria, MGO interacts with and modifies certain proteins, but the bacterial targets and effects of these modifications are not known. There is growing evidence demonstrating the importance of MGO in both host and microbial physiology, therefore understanding how bacteria cope with exposure to this molecule may inform the development of strategies to combat bacterial infections or modulate the human microbiome.

activity_code: F31
title: Assessing the spatial and temporal scales of attention effects and attention-dependent cholinergic release in macque V4.
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Attention importantly constrains vision, filtering sensory information so the brain only receives what it needs to act. But its mechanisms have been enigmatic since its formalization by William James (1890). My project will be the first to rigorously test the validity of the pervading hypothesis that release of the neuromodulator acetylcholine supports attention. Results from this will deepen our understanding of the core principles governing vision, cortical information encoding and representation, and neurological diseases in which attention is disrupted (e.g., schizophrenia, dementia), where interventions affecting cholinergic signaling have had variable results.

application_id: 11046496
activity_code: F31
title: Effects of the NR2E1 mutation on cellular and molecular determinants of neurogenesis
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative The decline of neurogenesis has been associated with many neurological disorders. Considering the critical role NR2E1, a nuclear receptor, plays in regulating neurogenesis, it is likely that certain NR2E1 missense mutations might cause neurogenesis-associated pathology by impairing NR2E1 function and/or activity. Our project will investigate the molecular, cellular, and behavioral consequences of one patient-specific NR2E1 mutation to gain insight into the importance of NR2E1 for human neurogenesis.

application_id: 11195519
activity_code: F32
title: Defining the link between trisomy 21 and placentation defects
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: Project Narrative
Proper establishment of a functional placenta is integral to the short and long-term health of a person. People
with Down’s Syndrome develop abnormal placentas and are coincidentally at an increased risk for chronic
diseases that are closely associated with placental insufficiency. We hypothesize that the placenta is in part
responsible for heart defects in people living with Down’s Syndrome and aim to identify a mechanism by which
the placenta can serve as an avenue for therapeutic intervention.

title: Sleep Characteristics in Children and Adolescents with Prenatal Alcohol Exposure
org_name: SAN DIEGO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Prenatal alcohol exposure (PAE) can cause a wide range of detrimental effects, such as neurobehavioral and functional impairments, that create challenges for caregivers, schools, and society at large. application_id: 10900172
activity_code: F30
title: Boosting protein synthesis using antisense oligonucleotides to treat neurodegeneration
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Safe and effective gene upregulation approaches in the central nervous system have the potential to treat many neurological diseases, including disorders of genetic haploinsufficiency and neurodegeneration. In the present study, we introduce a novel strategy using antisense oligonucleotides to stimulate protein synthesis and evaluate its applicability to ~40% of genes in the human genome, with a focus on proteins that could protect against neurodegenerative disease. Our findings would introduce a new class of gene-boosting biologics that are widely applicable, effective, and safely administered to the central nervous system.

application_id: 10948863
activity_code: K99
title: Learning features of optimal CAR T cells for LBCL from patient data
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cell therapies have revolutionized treatment options for patients with hematologic malignancies and hold promise for other types of cancer. However, limited CAR T cell expansion, persistence, and immune suppression from T regulatory (Treg) cells limit efficacy of CAR T cell therapies for large B cell lymphoma (LBCL). This proposal will: (1) employ fate mapping to identify drivers of desirable T cell states in pre-manufacture apheresis and infusion products with optimal expansion and persistence properties, and (2) mechanistically dissect and evaluate methods to overcome Treg suppression, providing a platform to improve CAR T cell therapy treatment options for patients with cancer.

title: Disentangling the role of poverty-related experiences on children's self-regulation: Identifying dynamic and contextually-relevant mechanisms
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Child poverty in combination with the enduring and inequitable burden of the COVID-19 pandemic underscores the urgent importance of understanding the effects of poverty on diverse children and families. Although many children flourish despite the stress that economic hardship brings, chronic and unpredictable poverty-related adversities put children at an increased risk of developing cognitive, emotional, and behavioral difficulties that, while potentially adaptive in the immediate context of poverty, may disrupt their capacity to meet the learning and psychosocial demands of formal schooling. application_id: 10895801
activity_code: F32
title: Osseoperception, Proprioception, and Postural Control in Individuals with an Osseointegrated Prosthesis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Individuals with transfemoral amputation fall significantly more often than the general population, and when fitted with a traditional prosthetic limb, nearly one-third of people experience severe complications with their prosthetic socket. Today, a secondary procedure called osseointegration is offered to address socket-related issues with a direct bone-anchored prosthesis implant, which offers a novel potential benefit to balance through improved sensation and body control. Understanding the effects of an osseointegrated prosthesis on sensation and postural control will help to identify specific functional movement deficiencies to target during therapy and rehabilitation to decrease fall risk.

application_id: 10902690
activity_code: F32
title: Walking Inefficiency in Multiple Sclerosis: Influence of Vascular Dysfunction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative The ability for blood vessels to dilate for the delivery of oxygen (O2) to targeted muscles and the extraction of O2 at the muscle level are impaired in persons with multiple sclerosis (MS); however, no research has examined the influence of lower-limb vascular function on poor walking efficiency in this population. This project may identify specific vascular mechanisms that contribute to walking efficiency in persons with MS. Such findings may guide researchers, clinicians, and exercise specialists in the design of targeted approaches to improve walking efficiency and its consequences with the aim of promoting health, managing comorbidities, and enhancing overall well-being, quality of life, and participation in the community.

rehabilitation to decrease fall risk.

activity_code: F31
title: Role of Vascular Chemerin as a Regulator of Blood Pressure and Contributor to Cardiovascular Disease
org_name: HENRY FORD HEALTH + MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Development of effective therapies for obesity-associated hypertension have been hindered by our limited understanding of the mechanisms by which excess adiposity drives cardiovascular disease (CVD). This fellowship proposal tests how the adipokine chemerin that is produced in the vasculature supports blood pressure and drives adiposity-associated hypertension and vascular stiffening. Through completion of this project, the applicant will develop key technical skills to highlight chemerin as a therapeutic target in obesity-associated hypertension and CVD as well as professional skills that will prepare her for a successful career as a clinical industrial scientist.

application_id: 11128442
activity_code: T32
title: Training grant in hemostasis and thrombosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
For over 20 years this T32 training grant has supported pre- and post-doctoral trainees interested in
hemostasis and thrombosis, areas with great clinical importance as heart attacks and strokes remain the
number one cause of mortality in the western world. We are requesting renewal to maintain our rich history of
mentoring in this space which is supported by an extra-ordinarily strong environment with an interactive and
dedicated group of innovative and experienced faculty members. The renewed T32 will provide excellent
research opportunities, while providing trainees with outstanding scientific development, mentoring, career
development, and training in career growth to ensure that there is a next generation of scientists interested in
the blood sciences.

application_id: 10864817
activity_code: K00
title: The regulation of innate immune sensors to control GVHD and GVL after allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) remains the major non-relapse complication after allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantion (aHSCTs) for hematologic cancers. Using pre-clinical models, this proposal will identify how innate immune sensors are activated during aHSCT and how the resulting pro-inflammatory signals contribute to GVHD. The proposed research ultimately aims to develop a strategy to prevent or reduce clinical GVHD while maintaining anti-tumor immunity to prevent leukemic relapse.

application_id: 10997256
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Role of Systemic Sex Hormones on Photoreceptor Degeneration
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposal investigates the molecular and transcriptional basis for how systemic circulating sex hormones are responsible for increased photoreceptor cell degeneration in a preclinical mouse model of Retinitis Pigmentosa, a group of devastating visual disorders leading to blindness. Specifically, I aim to determine how estrogen and progesterone signal through receptors in the retina to increase the photoreceptor stress responses and programmed cell death pathways. The results from this work will provide critical insight into treatment and monitoring strategies for male and female patients with these diseases, as well as potential approaches for future therapeutics targeting the sex hormone pathways in the retina during retinal degenerative disease.

and advance care planning discussions are usually a solitary event, we will design and evaluate a shared
