title: Epigenetic regulation of the regenerating axolotl forelimb's proximodistal axis by retinoic acid
org_name: NORTHEASTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The axolotl salamander is able to perfectly regenerate complex structures, including whole limbs. title: Mapping the functional GPCR-ome and its signaling consequences in neurons
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed studies aim to elucidate the chemical logic of neuromodulation by determining the implications of

communication. The cellular understanding of neuromodulation gained will have important translational
ramifications as neuromodulation is key to unlocking the brain in healthy and diseased states.

application_id: 10901645
activity_code: F32
title: Mapping the functional GPCR-ome and its signaling consequences in neurons
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative The proposed studies aim to elucidate the chemical logic of neuromodulation by determining the implications of co-expression of receptors in primary neurons and intact brain tissue. This proposal utilizes the latest tools in transcriptomics, genetics, and live microscopy to better understand the diversity of cell types and neuron communication. The cellular understanding of neuromodulation gained will have important translational ramifications as neuromodulation is key to unlocking the brain in healthy and diseased states.

title: Vision Research Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal seeks support for a program at the University of Michigan to train graduate
students and postdoctoral fellows in vision research. Research performed by doctoral students
and postdoctoral fellows supported by this training grant directly addresses problems in basic
and translational science with important implications for understanding normal biological
processes that impact human vision and diseases that result in vision loss. Training students
and postdoctoral fellows adds to the workforce of outstanding vision scientists in academic and
applied research and advances our knowledge in areas that are important for improving human
health.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE) are major pathogens among lung/liver transplant recipients,
who continue to experience infections years after transplant. I hypothesize that chronic CRE colonization and
late-onset infection are caused by persistence of CRE that colonize the gut early after transplant, a process
which is associated with a state of chronic gut dysbiosis and the emergence of antibiotic tolerance. I will
longitudinally characterize serial CRE isolates from patients and study the gut microbiome environment in which
CRE colonization, persistence, and infection occur, which will lead to future studies evaluating the mechanisms
of tolerance among CRE isolates after transplant, and of strategies to modify the microbiome of transplant
recipients with C...

activity_code: F32
title: Norepinephrine-sensing habenular microglia and stress-related behaviors
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE & PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE
Chronic stress contributes to many neuropsychiatric disorders, leads to cellular adaptations in the brain, and it
can influence other organs throughout the body via the release of stress hormones and norepinephrine. This
proposal aims to elucidate how norepinephrine signaling in microglia remodels neuron activity in response to
chronic and acute stress using a combination of molecular, imaging, and behavioral approaches. As such, the
results of this work will shed light on microglia-neuron interactions during stress and could reveal new strategies
to develop novel therapeutics for stress-related disorders such as anxiety, depression, or post-traumatic stress
disorder.

activity_code: F32
title: Evolutionary expansion of human cerebral cortex: spatial patterns and transcriptional correlates
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Portions of human cerebral cortex are greatly expanded relative to non-human primates and while this expansion likely underpins the unique capabilities that humans possess, it may also enable neuropsychiatric diseases, such as Alzheimer's dementia and schizophrenia, that have only limited analogs in non-human species. We propose to accurately map evolutionary expansion with state-of-the-art interspecies registration and identify the gene expression and cell types that drive these patterns. This research will improve the translatability of studies using non-human primate models, enrich our understanding of how the complex behaviors that make us human arose, and provide insight into predominately human mental and neurological disorders.

application_id: 11009322
activity_code: F32
title: Training in Behavioral Design Interventions to Address Stigma among Men Who have Sex with Men
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: In Malaysia, the high HIV prevalence among men who have sex with men (MSM) is exacerbated by significant stigma, hindering effective HIV prevention and treatment. This research proposal explores the implementation of rapid-start antiretroviral therapy (RS-ART) to reduce stigma and improve treatment outcomes for MSM. The study will employ asynchronous online focus groups and facilitate process improvement with clinical staff using NIATx quality improvement to assess RS-ART's effectiveness as a stigma reduction intervention, with dyadic analysis conducted to gain skills for a future K01.

title: Role of FOXK subfamily in KSHV infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV) is an oncogenic gammaherpesvirus that's main route of transmission occurs through saliva in the oral cavity. KSHV encodes an immediate early virion-associated tegument protein, that is vital for efficient lytic reactivation and virus production and that we have established interaction with key host transcription factors. application_id: 11072530
activity_code: F32
title: Long-term neural consequences of altered critical period timing.
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Developmental critical periods are brief windows in development when animal experience drives activity- dependent remodeling, or plasticity, within brain circuits. Critical period plasticity is terminated by the association of neurons and a specialized glial population called astrocytes, and changes in astrocyte-neuron signaling can extend or contract neural plasticity. However, the consequences of altered critical period timing are still poorly defined. The proposed project will identify the long-term consequences of extending or precociously closing a critical period of motor circuit plasticity, which will provide valuable insight into how circuit structure and dynamics are linked to motor behavior.

application_id: 10901131
activity_code: F31
title: Modeling pain variability in fibromyalgia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Fibromyalgia is a debilitating chronic pain condition; although pain is persistent, the severity typically varies across time, making it difficult to effectively manage. Despite this, intra-individual pain variability is rarely studied in fibromyalgia, even though pain variability has been associated with a host of negative outcomes in chronic pain conditions more broadly. This project will apply and develop advanced methodologies within pain research to characterize pain variability in fibromyalgia patients to predict clinically relevant outcomes and ultimately construct individualized pain profiles that can be used in a precision medicine context to target fibromyalgia treatments more effectively.

org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES

application_id: 11140977
activity_code: K23
title: Vaginal Microbiome and Metabolites in Preterm Birth
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Bacterial vaginosis (BV), an alteration in the vaginal microbiome, nearly doubles the risk of preterm birth (PTB),
delivery prior to 37 weeks, and affects one third of women of reproductive age. In the US, women with HIV
experience a two-fold increased risk of PTB compared to women without HIV and have been excluded from
studies investigating the association between vaginal dysbiosis and preterm birth, contributing to ongoing
disparities in this population. The proposed research will explore the role of vaginal dysbiosis in PTB among
women with HIV with the vision that vaginal dysbiosis will be associated with a pro-inflammatory milieu, with
altered cytokine and short chain fatty acid profiles, thus increasing the risk for PTB.

application_id: 11223305
activity_code: F32
title: Development of Capillary-on-Chip for the Study of Preservation Injury on Microvascular Endothelial Cells
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
There is a critical need to improve organ preservation times for organ transplant, but unfortunately, many of the
current protocols lead to damage of the endothelium. In the proposed project a novel microfluidic vasculature
model consisting of primary microvascular endothelial cells will be developed and compared to a rat hindlimb
machine perfusion model to understand the role of preservation injury in the microvasculature and how it can be
prevented. This will lead to an increase in endothelial focused preservation therapies and subsequently increase
the preservation potential of vascular composite allografts, along with other organs such as heart, lung, and
kidney, expanding the potential donor pool and patient access to organ transplantation.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative
This proposal aims to bridge the gap between preclinical and clinical osteoarthritis (OA) research by evaluating
pain-like behaviors in both rodent models and human patients. We will investigate gait compensations,

application_id: 10998090
activity_code: F32
title: The Effects of Ozone-Induced Oxysterols on Thrombotic Risk
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative Almost a third of the US population is exposed to unsafe ground ozone (O3) levels that negatively impact human respiratory and cardiovascular health through mechanisms that have not been entirely elucidated. The proposed work seeks to understand the role of O3-induced oxysterols on thrombosis by evaluating the hypercoagulability of plasma from O3 exposed subjects via thrombin generation and determining the effects of O3-induced oxysterols on endothelial cell activation. Together, data from this proposal will inform how O3 exposure and O3-induced oxysterols might impact cardiovascular disease by identifying specific biomarkers and mechanisms to clearly link oxidant air pollutant exposure with thrombotic risk.

application_id: 11076624
activity_code: F31
title: Origins of sex differences in the mechanisms of obesity-associated hypertension
org_name: AUGUSTA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite the longstanding dogma that premenopausal women are commonly viewed as protected from
hypertension and cardiovascular disease (CVD), compelling evidence establishes that obesity abrogates the
protective effects of female sex and predisposes women to obesity-associated hypertension (OAH) though,
through sex specific mechanisms. The source of this sex difference in mechanisms is unknown. The proposed
study aims to uncover the underlying origin of the mechanisms for sex differences in OAH by investigating role
for sex chromosome complement and will test whether human female adrenals are more sensitive to leptin-
mediated aldosterone production than that of males.

title: Functional interrogation of region-specific reactive astrocyte factors in neuroinflammation
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Inflammation is ubiquitous in all damage and disease in the central nervous system. Astrocytes in the brain and spinal cord become reactive in response to inflammation, losing key homeostatic functions and gaining harmful functions which may contribute to the progression of neurological disease and degeneration. While previously thought to be a homogenous response, our preliminary data indicates striking brain region differences in inflammatory astrocyte reactivity, and the proposed research will investigate the significance of two region-specific reactive astrocyte factors in neuro-immune interactions, thermoregulation, and behavior.

application_id: 11211152
activity_code: F32
title: Thalamocortical Circuits Governing Egocentric-Allocentric Spatial Reference Frame Transformations
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research will characterize the flow of visuospatial information along a circuit involving the rodent
pulvinar, postrhinal cortex, and medial entorhinal cortex that transforms high-dimensional sensory information
into straightforward spatial representations that can guide spatial memory and behavior. Dysfunction in these
brain regions is associated with cognitive impairments in disorders such as Alzheimer’s disease and
topographical disorientation. Understanding the precise functional connections among these regions may
provide insight into mechanisms underlying these disorders and will significantly expand our understanding of
the neural processing of space.

title: Neuropeptide Y1 Receptor-Expressing Neurons in the Lateral Parabrachial Nucleus in Neuropathic Pain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative Neuropathic pain is a debilitating form of chronic pain afflicting millions of patients worldwide but is poorly responsive to current analgesic therapies. Recent preclinical behavioral data implicates the lateral parabrachial nucleus of the brainstem in the maintenance of both the behavioral reflexive and affective components of neuropathic pain. This study uses in vivo and awake moving fiber photometry and in situ fluorescent hybridization, intraparabrachial pharmacology and chemogenetics, and anatomical tracing combined with efferent terminal chemogenetic inhibition to ultimately determine if excitatory lateral parabrachial neurons (those that contain the type 1 receptor for neuropeptide Y) contribute to the behavioral manifestations of neuropathic pain.

application_id: 11074080
activity_code: F32
title: Hippocampal Sharp-Wave Ripple and Replay Mechanisms Underlying Long-Term Memory
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project narrative
These studies will use two-photon calcium imaging, single-cell photostimulation, and electrophysiology methods
in vivo to investigate how hippocampal sharp-wave ripples and replay events shape and stabilize memory
ensembles. Results from this proposed study will provide valuable insight on the population-wide and microcircuit
mechanisms underlying long-term memory. By advancing our knowledge of the neural mechanisms of long-term
memory in hippocampal networks, we can develop more effective treatments and interventions for memory-
impaired clinical populations, such as patients of Alzheimer’s disease or dementia.

application_id: 10826026
activity_code: F32
title: Stereochemical Editing of Quaternary Stereocenters Enabled by Enantioselective Recombination of C–C Bonds
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Stereochemistry is the crux of modern organic chemistry; therefore, direct synthetic method to access desired stereoisomer is highly sought after from fundamental organic synthesis to medicinal chemistry perspective. This proposed research describes a novel application of enantioselective photoredox catalysis to achieve stereochemical editing of quaternary stereocenters that is not established with current methods. Ultimately, the results of this proposed research will provide valuable tools to efficiently prepare small molecule drugs and their derivatives, and enhance the productivity of pharmaceutical research to advance human health.

title: Caregiver influences on item-context memory and arousal in toddlers
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Memories from early childhood do not persist into adulthood – a phenomenon that remains a neurobiological puzzle.
This proposal aims to examine item-context memory and arousal in toddlers, and how it is modulated by caregivers,
in order to advance understanding of the functions and plasticity of developing learning and memory systems in
toddlers. In the long-term, this work has implications for why infant/toddler memories are so fragile and fleeting, yet
at the same time, early caregiving experiences have enduring effects on cognition and socioemotional outcomes.

title: The role of iron in retinal degeneration during bacterial infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Endophthalmitis, a bacterial infection inside the eye, can quickly lead to permanent vision loss. Bacteria need

application_id: 11054542
activity_code: F32
title: Elucidating the role of interferon gamma production and signaling in the generation of neuropathic pain in human dorsal root ganglia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS DALLAS
phr: Project Narrative In individuals with neuropathic pain, females show increased expression of interferon gamma (IFNγ) induced genes in their dorsal root ganglia (hDRG) suggesting a potential sex specific mechanism in the production of pain. To elucidate which cells in hDRG are capable of releasing IFNγ, I will isolate each immune cell type from hDRGs of both sexes and perform transcriptomic and proteomic assessments looking for IFNγ genes and proteins. Then I will expose cultured hDRG neurons of both sexes to IFNγ and perform a comprehensive analysis looking at the functional, transcriptomic, and proteomic changes that occur to determine what alterations IFNγ could be causing at the hDRG to produce neuropathic pain.

title: Characterizing dopaminergic systems changing exploratory behavior in hungry animals
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Narrative: The research proposed in this project will employ cutting-edge behavioral analysis tools and neural recording and manipulation techniques to test how caloric need may drive changes in exploratory behavior and associated dopaminergic circuitry. title: Investigating the role of Wnt signaling effectors on the neuron-glia switch and OPC specification during development and after injury.

activity_code: T32
title: Basic Medical Research Training in Oncology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: The objective of the T32 training program at the University of Chicago is to recruit, retain and provide an exceptional research training environment for postdoctoral fellows who have completed internal medicine training from the most competitive residency training programs. By fostering strong mentor-mentee relationships, the program prepares the next generation of competent, creative and compassionate physicians for research-intensive careers in oncology. Physicians completing our training program will enter the oncology work force ready to lead patient centric research teams across the cancer care continuum from prevention, early diagnosis, treatment and survivorship in academia, government or industry.

application_id: 10994269
activity_code: F30
title: S. aureus COG0523s as bacterial zinc metallochaperones critical to pathogenesis
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Staphylococcus aureus is a Gram-positive pathogen that relies on nutrient metals to respond to stress and cause infections. The proposed research will define the importance of zinc during S. aureus pathogenesis by determining the function of two putative metallochaperones CrzA and ZigA that we hypothesize bind and activate zinc-dependent S. aureus proteins implicated in DNA maintenance and DNA damage repair. The proposed work will establish the importance of CrzA- and ZigA-dependent metal allocation during the pathogenesis of infection and establish the regulation of DNA repair as a target for the development of new anti-microbial agents.

application_id: 10766267
activity_code: K01
title: Mechanisms of thymic stromal-immune crosstalk for homeostasis and naïve T cell licensing across life
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Reduced T cell mediated immunity is a prominent feature of aging with important clinical consequences and is partially driven by diminished thymic function and altered naïve T cell development. Recent evidence suggests heterogeneity amongst naïve T cells that are generated during different developmental windows, but little is known about the intrathymic crosstalk that imprints naïve T cell character. This proposal focuses on understanding the novel role of epithelial-derived IL-13 in establishing medullary thymic function during early adulthood and how these pathways change during thymic aging, which may inform efforts to protect existing thymic function or reverse thymic involution to maximize T cell mediated immunity in the elderly.

title: A Comparative Analysis of OPC and Microglia Phagocytosis and its Relevance to Neurodegenerative Disease
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The overarching goal of this proposal is to gain a deeper understanding of OPC phagocytosis in both health and disease
and determine how phagocytosis alters their differentiation capacity. These results will have implications for
neurodegenerative disease as lack of OPC differentiation is observed in multiple neurodegenerative states. This data will
lay critical groundwork for future investigation into the diverse functions of OPCs in disease and will provide novel
insight into new mechanisms that may alter OPC differentiation.
